questions.critique: While there is no single test to diagnose polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), the diagnosis can be made based on the presence of at least 2 of the 3 Rotterdam criteria: oligoanovulation, hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovaries on ultrasonography. Other conditions that may mimic PCOS must be excluded, including an androgen-secreting tumor, exogenous androgen exposure, Cushing syndrome, nonclassical congenital adrenal hyperplasia, primary hypothalamic amenorrhea, primary ovarian insufficiency, acromegaly, thyroid disease, prolactin disorders, and genetic disorders that impede insulin action. Hyperandrogenism may be diagnosed clinically with evidence of hirsutism, androgenic alopecia, acne, or seborrhea. Additionally, it may be diagnosed biochemically with elevated androgen levels, such as an elevated free testosterone level. An elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone level would not suggest PCOS, but rather a possible diagnosis of congenital adrenal hyperplasia, which may share overlapping clinical features with PCOS. An elevated cortisol level would similarly suggest an etiology other than PCOS, such as Cushing syndrome or adrenal tumor, which may also share overlapping clinical features with PCOS. Although insulin resistance is often part of the complex pathophysiology of PCOS, neither elevated insulin nor glucose levels are part of the Rotterdam criteria for PCOS.

questions.stem: A generally healthy 36-year-old male presents to your clinic with a 36-hour history of dizziness and nausea. He says he feels as though the world is spinning and he tends to fall to the left when he tries to walk. The episodes of dizziness occur spontaneously and can last from minutes to hours. He does not have any recent illness, hearing loss, or tinnitus, and he does not take any medications. An HEENT examination is normal. On neurologic testing, he exhibits horizontal nystagmus with left lateral gaze. Head-impulse testing reveals a rapid movement of both eyes. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step?

options.D: Valacyclovir (Valtrex), 1000 mg twice daily for 7 days

questions.critique: This patient's vertigo is caused by vestibular neuritis, as evidenced by its spontaneity, rotatory vertigo, nausea, and listing to the affected side with walking. In this scenario, head-impulse testing shows saccade, and the head-impulse, nystagmus, test of skew (HINTS) examination is significant for horizontal nystagmus to the affected side. Vestibular neuritis is most often found in patients 30–50 years of age and is thought to have a viral etiology. It involves prolonged, continuous vertigo, while benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) is characterized by brief, episodic vertigo associated with head movements. Vestibular neuritis usually resolves on its own within a few days, although some patients have recurrent symptoms for months. The treatment for vestibular neuritis is reassurance. Antiemetics can be used for up to 3 days. Patients with symptoms that last longer should be referred for vestibular rehabilitation. Home Epley maneuvers are appropriate for vertigo caused by BPPV, but are neither indicated nor effective for vestibular neuritis (SOR A). Corticosteroids and antiviral medications have not been shown to be effective in treating vestibular neuritis. MRI of the brain is indicated for patients who exhibit signs of central vertigo or those with hearing loss (SOR C).

questions.stem: A 24-year-old gravida 2 para 2 presents to your office with melasma that has not resolved since she gave birth 6 months ago. She is eager to be treated. Which one of the following topical treatments would be the most effective for this patient's melasma?

options.E: A combination of fluocinolone, hydroquinone, and tretinoin

questions.critique: Melasma is a common condition in pregnancy that occurs in up to 75% of patients. It is caused by increased levels of progesterone, estrogen, and melanocyte-stimulating hormone. While it resolves in 90% of cases during the postpartum period, it can persist and may recur in subsequent pregnancies or with the use of oral contraception. Topical tretinoin, hydroquinone, and a corticosteroid such as fluocinolone can all be used in the treatment of melasma, and a combination of the 3 is the most effective. These may be prescribed as a single formulation, which may be cost prohibitive, or as individual components. Azelaic acid and benzoyl peroxide are used to treat acne and are not effective for treating melasma. Imiquimod is an immunomodulator that is helpful in the treatment of condyloma acuminata but has no role in the treatment of melasma.

questions.stem: You see a newborn for a routine wellness check. The parents tell you that there is a family history of allergies to eggs and peanuts, and they ask how to reduce their child's risk of developing these food allergies. Evidence supports exposing the patient to these potentially allergenic foods starting at which one of the following ages?
questions.critique: The lowest risk of developing future allergies to many foods, including peanuts and eggs, occurs with early exposure at 4–6 months of age. Peanut butter can be thinned with water, breast milk, cow’s milk, or formula. Peanuts should not be given in their original hard form because it is a choking hazard. Eggs should be cooked well prior to eating.

questions.stem: A 23-year-old female presents after a sexual assault that occurred the prior evening. She reports nonconsensual penetrative vaginal intercourse. The assailant was an acquaintance with unknown medical history. The patient takes no medications and is up to date on vaccinations, including tetanus, HPV, and hepatitis B, although her hepatitis B serology status is unknown. She elects to undergo a medical forensic examination, which is completed with the help of a certified Sexual Assault Nurse Examiner. A urine pregnancy test is negative, and sexually transmitted infection testing is performed. Which one of the following would be most appropriate to offer at this time?
questions.critique: In addition to trauma-informed evaluation and counseling, women who experience sexual assault should be offered forensic examination, emergency contraception, and screening and prevention for sexually transmitted infections. Empiric treatment for Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Chlamydia trachomatis, and Trichomonas vaginalis is appropriate and should include ceftriaxone, azithromycin, and either metronidazole or tinidazole. Postexposure prophylaxis for HIV should be made available with triple therapy for 28 days, rather than the double therapy regimens used in pre-exposure prophylaxis. Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir is used to treat hepatitis C infection, but routine postexposure prophylaxis is not recommended. Long-term contraception may be offered, but patients should first be provided with effective emergency contraception, such as ulipristal, levonorgestrel, or the copper IUD.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old female presents with a long-standing history of episodes of depressed mood, headaches, and irritability that occur in the 1–2 weeks preceding her menses. She has a history of anxiety that is well controlled on fluoxetine (Prozac), 60 mg daily. She has a copper IUD (Paragard) for contraception. She has tried combined oral contraceptives to control the premenstrual symptoms, but found that they worsened her irritability. Which one of the following treatments would be most effective for this patient's symptoms?
questions.critique: Symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS) with mood instability can be reduced with 1000–1200 mg of calcium carbonate daily (SOR B). Patients with PMS symptoms have been found to have cyclic changes in calcium levels that may account for the symptoms, but the mechanism of action is still unknown. All SSRIs are first-line treatments for symptoms of PMS and premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD), but bupropion has not been shown to be effective. Continuous combined oral contraceptives are also first-line treatment (SOR A). High-dose dutasteride (2.5 mg daily) has been shown to alleviate PMDD symptoms, but it can have a deleterious effect on mood and is teratogenic. Low-dose dutasteride has not demonstrated beneficial effect on PMDD symptoms. Patients with PMS have not been found to have low levels of vitamin D, and supplementing with vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) has not been clearly demonstrated to alleviate PMS symptoms. There is insufficient evidence to prove the benefit of acupuncture in reducing PMS symptoms.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old female with osteoporosis (T-score –2.8) is seen in your office for the first time. She was prescribed alendronate (Fosamax), 70 mg weekly, by her previous physician. The patient took the medication but discontinued it after 4 weeks because of gastrointestinal symptoms. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate action at this time?

options.B: Instructing the patient to take the medication before bed
options.C: Instructing the patient to take the medication with food
options.D: Discontinuing the medication and starting raloxifene (Evista)
options.E: Discontinuing the medication and arranging for intravenous bisphosphonates

questions.critique: Women ≥65 years of age should be tested for osteoporosis. A bone mineral density score ≤–2.5 indicates the need for treatment, as seen in this patient. Oral bisphosphonates, such as alendronate, are considered first-line treatment. To minimize gastrointestinal irritation, oral bisphosphonates should be taken on an empty stomach with 8 oz of water, and the patient should remain upright and avoid food and beverages other than plain water for at least 30 minutes afterward. If oral bisphosphonates are not tolerated despite correct administration, intravenous bisphosphonates should be considered. In this case, the patient should discontinue alendronate and transition to intravenous bisphosphonate therapy. Raloxifene is not the preferred initial treatment.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old patient presents to the emergency department with a fever, headache, and stiff neck. You diagnose sepsis and perform a lumbar puncture. The results are consistent with bacterial meningitis. In addition to antibiotics, which one of the following intravenous therapies is most likely to improve mortality?
questions.critique: Despite optimal therapy, the mortality and loss of quality-adjusted life-years from bacterial meningitis is significant. Empiric therapy for adults includes vancomycin and ceftriaxone. Intravenous (IV) dexamethasone, when given shortly before or at the time of administration of IV antibiotics, has been shown to improve mortality (odds ratio 0.90) and neurologic sequelae. Bactericidal antibiotics help to reduce inflammation. Acyclovir would be indicated if there is clinical concern for herpes simplex virus or symptoms of encephalitis. While optimal fluid management for meningitis should be tailored based on clinical evaluation, this patient has also been diagnosed with sepsis and should receive appropriate fluid resuscitation; therefore, IV furosemide would be inappropriate. Glycerol is a hyperosmolar agent intended to reduce intracranial pressure in bacterial meningitis, but it cannot be recommended for treatment due to an increased risk of death according to available data in adults with a high prevalence of HIV. IV immunoglobulin is not a recognized treatment for bacterial meningitis.

questions.stem: A 33-year-old male presents for follow-up of long-standing heartburn. His symptoms have been present for 3 years and recent treatment for 1 month with pantoprazole (Protonix) has only provided partial relief. He has a history of allergic rhinitis that is treated with fluticasone propionate nasal spray (Flonase). He has had dysphagia with solids, and a food impaction 3 months ago that resolved spontaneously. Upper endoscopy reveals eosinophilic esophagitis. Which one of the following is most likely to improve this patient’s symptoms?
questions.critique: Eosinophilic esophagitis is an inflammatory condition triggered by food antigens in sensitive individuals. Endoscopic findings of linear grooves, concentric rings, and whitish exudates may suggest its presence, but the diagnosis is confirmed histologically based on the presence of >15 eosinophils/hpf and the exclusion of other causes of eosinophilia. The diagnosis should be suspected in patients with heartburn who have atopic illnesses such as allergic rhinitis, asthma, or eczema and who do not respond to proton pump inhibitor therapy. Eosinophilic esophagitis is more common in men in their 20s and 30s, and dysphagia may be present for 4 or more years before diagnosis. More than half of patients with this condition may have a history of food impaction. First-line pharmacologic treatment is oral budesonide. Alternatively, inhalant corticosteroids may be sprayed into the oral cavity during a breath hold and then swallowed to minimize pulmonary deposition. Treatment with proton pump inhibitors may help, but they do not provide complete relief in two-thirds of cases. Dietary changes can be very helpful, but consultation with an allergist may be needed. Immunosuppressed individuals such as patients with HIV or diabetes mellitus may have Candida esophagitis, which is treated with fluconazole rather than clotrimazole. Adding an H2-blocker such as famotidine would help in cases of erosive esophagitis. Metoclopramide was previously used to treat severe GERD, but it is no longer encouraged due to side effects. While montelukast is used to treat asthma and allergic rhinitis, it is not as effective for eosinophilic esophagitis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old male with a recent diagnosis of Crohn disease presents to your office for a health maintenance examination. His gastroenterologist advised that he ask you about vaccine updates. He has not seen a primary care physician in over 15 years. His current medications include methotrexate and azathioprine (Imuran). He received a Tdap vaccine 6 years ago at an urgent care clinic and recently received a seasonal influenza vaccine and an updated COVID-19 vaccine at his gastroenterologist’s office. He is confident that he received all of his childhood vaccines. Which one of the following vaccines should you recommend today?
questions.critique: Adults with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) should receive a pneumococcal vaccine. Meningococcal vaccine is currently recommended for adolescents with IBD. The MMR vaccine is contraindicated in patients already on immunosuppressive treatment. Because this patient's Tdap vaccine was administered <10 years ago, he is not due for another dose. Similarly, his next dose of zoster vaccine will be due when he is 50 years of age, and there is no recommendation to administer the next vaccine before the age of 50 in patients with IBD.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old college student with a recent history of sinusitis and a past medical history of depression, adrenal insufficiency, and hypothyroidism is brought to the urgent care clinic by her roommates after she experienced a syncopal episode. The patient reports progressive weakness and fatigue, increasingly severe abdominal pain, and repeated episodes of nausea and vomiting over the last few days. A review of her electronic health record shows the following medications: Amoxicillin/clavulanate (Augmentin) Escitalopram (Lexapro) Fludrocortisone Levothyroxine (Synthroid) Prednisolone Her vital signs at presentation include a blood pressure of 97/58 mm Hg, a pulse rate of 116 beats/min, and a temperature of 37.2 °C (99.0 °F). A basic metabolic panel is notable for the following results: Sodium 133 mEq/L (N 136–145) Potassium 5.8 mEq/L (N 3.4–4.4) Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (N 0.6–1.1) Glucose 60 mg/dL Which one of the following therapies should be started as soon as possible to prevent further worsening of this patient's acute condition?
questions.critique: This patient's chronic corticosteroid use, symptoms, and electrolyte abnormalities suggest she is experiencing an adrenal crisis; the correct initial treatment of this is intravenous hydrocortisone. Acute illness, dehydration, severe physical stress, or nonadherence to treatment are all possible causes of acute adrenal crisis in patients with chronic adrenal insufficiency. These patients may present with weakness, severe fatigue, abdominal pain, tachycardia, and hypotension. Laboratory abnormalities can include hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and, in some cases, hypoglycemia. If left untreated, adrenal crisis can be fatal. Glucagon is primarily used to treat hypoglycemia in patients with insulin-dependent diabetes, but it will not correct the cardiovascular abnormalities of adrenal crisis. Dexamethasone is a corticosteroid that can be used to treat adrenal crisis, but it is not the preferred therapy according to Endocrine Society practice guidelines. Additionally, a 1-mg dose is subtherapeutic for adrenal crisis. Dexamethasone is sometimes used at this dose to test for primary hyperadrenalism. Levofloxacin is not appropriate for treatment of this patient's adrenal crisis. Although ondansetron may help reduce vomiting, it will not correct the underlying cause of this patient's illness.

questions.stem: A 37-year-old female is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after collapsing while standing in line at a bookstore. Her friend witnessed the episode and describes it as "fainting." A history and physical examination were performed at the bedside while triaging the patient. The patient's past medical history and a physical examination, including vital signs and a neurologic assessment, are unremarkable. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient with suspected syncope?
questions.critique: Electrocardiography (EKG) is a primary evaluation tool, along with a comprehensive history and physical examination, to investigate the etiology of a syncopal episode (SOR C). Electroencephalography (EEG) is not recommended as a routine test. Carotid ultrasonography is not recommended for evaluation of syncope in patients with a normal physical examination (SOR B). Similarly, head CT and MRI are not recommended as neurologic imaging studies in patients with a normal neurologic examination after a syncopal episode (SOR B). If the patient were noted to experience syncope with exertion, an exercise stress test would be appropriate (SOR C). However, this patient was observed to be at rest when the "fainting" occurred.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old male presents with urinary frequency. His symptoms have been ongoing for months, but he says they have acutely worsened in the last 6–8 weeks. His symptoms are predominantly storage-related with daytime frequency, nocturia, urgency, and even infrequent incontinence. He also reports difficulty voiding, noting a weak stream and hesitancy. He does not have erectile dysfunction. Urinalysis is negative. In addition to behavioral and lifestyle alterations, which one of the following medications should you recommend for initial therapy?
questions.critique: Men with lower urinary tract symptoms may have overactive bladder, bladder outlet obstruction, or both. Differentiating overactive bladder and bladder outlet obstruction can be difficult due to significant symptom overlap. Overactive bladder is most often due to detrusor overactivity, and bladder outlet obstruction is most often secondary to benign prostatic hyperplasia. The most appropriate initial treatment for these symptoms is an α- blocker, such as tamsulosin, alfuzosin, or terazosin (Evidence Level A), regardless of the predominance of storage symptoms (eg, daytime frequency, nocturia, urgency, incontinence) or voiding symptoms (eg, weak stream, hesitancy, straining, dribbling). The American Urological Association recommends 5-α-reductase inhibitors if prostate volume is ≥30 mL on imaging, the prostate is enlarged on examination, or PSA levels exceed 1.5 ng/mL. 5-α-Reductase inhibitors can take up to a year to take full effect. Anticholinergics (oxybutynin) and β3-agonists (mirabegron) may be used alone or in combination with α-blockers for patients with predominantly storage-related symptoms if α-blockers are not adequately effective. Some guidelines recommend assessing postvoid residual volume before initiating these agents. A daily phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor (typically tadalafil) can be used as initial monotherapy or as combination therapy in patients with erectile dysfunction, although it is not generally recommended as first-line treatment. The mechanism of action is not fully understood.

questions.stem: A 5-day-old male is brought to your office by his mother for an initial well child visit. After an uncomplicated, full-term pregnancy, the mother gave birth 102 hours ago through vaginal delivery and was discharged 48 hours ago. She shares that this is her first child, and she is concerned that he is not getting enough milk despite exclusively breastfeeding 10–12 times per day. Which one of the following factors, if present, would indicate inadequate breastfeeding at this time?

options.C: Infant weight loss of 5% from birth weight
options.D: Maternal breasts that appear small and not full
options.E: Maternal nipple pain during the first 15 seconds of each feed

questions.critique: Recognition of adequate newborn feeding and parental lactation counseling is important in the care of mothers and infants. An infant who is feeding adequately should produce 6 or more wet diapers and 3 or more stools after 4 days of life. Infant weight loss is normal in the first 3 days of life, but a weight loss of more than 7% from birth weight or ongoing weight loss after 3 days of life should raise concern. Maternal breasts that appear smaller or less full do not necessarily indicate poor feeding; however, lack of any breast changes or leaking of milk by day 5 post partum may indicate a problem with milk supply. Maternal nipple pain is normal with breastfeeding, but pain that persists beyond the first 30–60 seconds should prompt evaluation and counseling. If supplementation with formula is deemed medically necessary, protocol from the Academy of Breastfeeding Medicine should be followed.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old male presents to the urgent care clinic with a 4-day history of fever, chills, myalgias, and cough. He lives with a roommate and works remotely from home. His last influenza vaccine was 5 years ago, and his most recent COVID-19 vaccine was 2 years ago. His temperature is 38.3 °C (100.9 °F), and his heart rate and blood pressure are within the normal range. A physical examination is normal except for occasional rhonchi. Rapid antigen testing is positive for influenza B and negative for influenza A and COVID-19. Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this patient's influenza B?
questions.critique: According to the CDC, antiviral treatment for influenza is not recommended for non–high- risk individuals who present more than 48 hours after symptom onset. Thus, supportive therapy is most important for this patient who is at low risk for hospitalization and death. Methylprednisolone is not recommended to treat influenza. The risk for hospitalization and death is not reduced in adults and children treated with a neuraminidase inhibitor in the outpatient setting, as supported by systematic and unpublished randomized clinical trials. While oseltamivir is recommended as first-line therapy for high-risk patients, it is not indicated for this patient, who has no underlying medical conditions or known exposure to high-risk individuals and presents more than 48 hours after symptom onset. Rimantadine is not active against influenza B and is also not considered first-line medical therapy for influenza.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old male with a long-standing history of obsessive-compulsive disorder presents for follow-up to discuss potential pharmacotherapy due to an inadequate treatment response to psychotherapy. Which one of the following medications would be most likely to improve this patient’s symptoms while minimizing the risk of adverse side effects?
questions.critique: The most effective medication therapy options for obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) are SSRIs. Fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, sertraline, and paroxetine all have approval from the FDA for the treatment of OCD. Dosages that are higher than usual are often required to achieve symptom control. Clomipramine, a tricyclic antidepressant, has an indication from the FDA for treatment of OCD, but a higher toxicity risk makes it less favorable for first-line treatment. Bupropion (a norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitor), buspirone (an atypical anxiolytic), and quetiapine (an atypical antipsychotic) are not first-line treatment for OCD.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old female with hypertension sees you for a routine follow-up visit. She fell about 2 weeks ago while hurrying to answer her home telephone, and sustained bruising and swelling of the left lower leg. She does not recall feeling dizzy or lightheaded before the fall, and she did not hit her head or lose consciousness. She was able to get up and walk afterward without any limitations but developed a large bruise over the lateral hip. She has not had any previous falls and she feels well today. She lives alone and performs activities of daily living independently. Which one of the following has the best evidence to prevent future falls and associated serious injury?

options.A: Patient education on fall harms and prevention
options.B: Use of a cane while walking
options.C: Referral to a group exercise program, including gait, balance, and functional training
options.D: Referral to an ophthalmologist for a comprehensive vision assessment
options.E: Referral to a podiatrist for footwear assessment

questions.critique: Falls are a common cause of serious injury and death in older adults in the United States. A 2018 study found that 27.5% of adults ≥65 years of age had fallen at least once in the past year (714 falls per 1000 community-dwelling older adults). More than 300,000 older adults are hospitalized each year for hip fracture, which is one of the most serious potential complications from falls in older adults. Falls are also the leading cause of injury-related death in older adults. A history of a fall is a key risk factor for future falls. Other risks include sensory and cognitive deficits, acute and chronic health conditions, alcohol and drug use, and certain other medication use. In community-dwelling older adults who are identified as being at higher risk of falls, the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force found that exercise interventions lower the risk of falls and should be recommended. Both individual and group exercise programs are beneficial and there is no data to recommend any single type of exercise over others. Multifactorial interventions to reduce falls typically include an assessment of modifiable risk factors followed by a customized approach to reducing risk. Evaluations may include balance, gait, vision, cognition, postural blood pressure, medication use, and the home environment. This assessment differs from a focused diagnostic assessment of a patient with a specific concern such as vision change, dizziness, or numbness in the feet. Interventions to address risk factors identified in a multifactorial screening program may offer benefit to high-risk individuals, although studies have found a small net benefit on average. A 2023 Cochrane review comprising 22 studies on environmental interventions found evidence of benefit for reducing fall hazards at home by using a validated tool to identify and address hazards, usually via a home visit. Patient education, assistive devices, vision impairment correction, and footwear selection were not found to be benefici

questions.stem: Which one of the following would be most appropriate for initial treatment of Helicobacter pylori infection?

options.A: Dual therapy with vonoprazan (Voquezna) and amoxicillin
options.B: Triple therapy with omeprazole, amoxicillin, and clarithromycin
options.C: Triple therapy with omeprazole, amoxicillin, and levofloxacin
options.D: Quadruple therapy with bismuth, omeprazole, metronidazole, and tetracycline

questions.critique: Infection with Helicobacter pylori should be treated when it causes symptoms. Several regimens exist for the treatment of H pylori infection. Guidelines currently recommend optimized quadruple therapy with bismuth, metronidazole, omeprazole, and tetracycline. This combination is approximately 91% effective at eradicating H pylori infection. Dual therapy with vonoprazan and amoxicillin is an option for patients who are unable or unwilling to take quadruple therapy, but its eradiation rates only range from 70% to 80%. Regimens containing clarithromycin or levofloxacin should be limited to infections with documented susceptibility to macrolides or fluoroquinolones, respectively.

questions.stem: The parent of a 5-year-old male calls your office after the child sustains burns to his arms and chest while helping his father boil pasta in the kitchen. The father tells you that the burned areas of skin are red without any open areas. He estimates the size of the burns on the arms at 2–3 cm in diameter and those on the chest at 5–6 cm. The child is crying in pain and the father asks what he should do. Which one of the following immediate interventions has the best evidence of reducing burn depth, improving healing time, and decreasing skin graft requirements?

options.A: Cooling the burn surfaces with running tap water
options.B: Applying ice to the burn surfaces
options.C: Covering the burn surfaces with cool, wet gauze
options.D: Applying aloe vera gel to the burn surfaces and leaving them uncovered
options.E: Applying diclofenac gel to the burn surfaces and leaving them uncovered

questions.critique: Burns are a common cause of injury in the United States, but most are minor and can be treated in the outpatient setting. The initial treatment goals for burn injuries include limiting the extent of the injury, cleaning the area, and managing pain. Removing clothing limits the injury related to scalding. The most effective way to limit the extent of injury is to cool the burn surface by running cool tap water over the burn surface for at least 20 minutes. This intervention has been shown to reduce burn depth, improve healing time, and decrease skin graft requirements. Ideally, burns should be cooled within the first 30 minutes of injury, but some evidence suggests benefit up to 3 hours after the initial injury. Applying ice to the burn surfaces is not recommended as a cooling strategy due to the risk of vasoconstriction, which can contribute to tissue damage. Wet dressings are not effective as a cooling strategy and are not recommended. For superficial burns such as those described in this patient, aloe vera and NSAIDs can be used to address pain after the initial cooling strategy, but these are not the recommended first step. Burns are classified by their depth: superficial (first degree), partial thickness (second degree), and full thickness (third degree). Superficial burns are limited to the epidermal layer. They are painful and appear red (blanchable), with intact skin and no blistering. Partial-thickness burns are deeper and involve injury to the dermis. Superficial partial- thickness burns may blister and be exudative, whereas deep partial-thickness burns are drier and do not blanch. Full-thickness burns involve the entire dermis and extend into the fat and connective tissue layers. They appear white rather than red, and are dry with a waxy appearance. Patients often experience less pain due to the destruction of nerve endings. Deep partial-thickness and full-thickness burns should be evaluated and treated at a burn center.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old female with a past medical history of obesity and cigarette smoking presents with a popliteal deep vein thrombosis of the right leg, confirmed on ultrasonography. She has no prior history of venous thromboembolism and has not had any recent surgery, trauma, or immobilization. She does not take any medications. Which one of the following anticoagulation regimens would be the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.D: LMWH for 7 days, followed by warfarin
options.E: Unfractionated heparin for 48 hours, followed by a DOAC

questions.critique: Direct-acting oral anticoagulants (DOACs) are now recommended as monotherapy for outpatient management of deep vein thrombosis. DOACs reach therapeutic levels quickly; therefore, concomitant initiation of unfractionated or low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) is not necessary. DOACs have less major bleeding and require less monitoring, making them preferred over warfarin. Previously, unfractionated heparin was administered during hospitalization while warfarin was simultaneously initiated; however, subcutaneous LMWH can now be started with warfarin as an outpatient and continued until the goal INR is reached if a DOAC is unaffordable.

questions.stem: Which one of the following puts a patient at the highest risk of developing breast cancer?

options.A: A first pregnancy at <30 years of age
options.B: Onset of menopause at <50 years of age
options.D: A grandmother with a history of breast cancer
options.E: Chest radiation therapy as a child

questions.critique: Women who received chest radiotherapy as children are at increased risk for breast cancer as adults, with those who received whole lung radiation at especially high risk. Other risk factors for breast cancer include the onset of menopause at >55 years of age, a first pregnancy at >35 years of age, and a first-degree relative with breast cancer. Patients with dense breasts have a higher amount of glandular and stromal tissue compared with adipose tissue on mammography and are at higher risk for breast cancer.

questions.stem: An 83-year-old female sees you for follow-up 2 weeks after being discharged home from an acute rehabilitation facility. She experienced an acute ischemic stroke 6 weeks ago and spent several weeks in the rehabilitation setting after an initial hospital stay. Home health services have been assisting her since she returned home. Today she reports improved mobility, speech, and swallowing, but persistent difficulties with low mood. She finds it difficult to enjoy hobbies and avoids visiting with family and friends. She has been sleeping more than usual and finds it difficult to get out of bed in the morning. You diagnose poststroke depression. In addition to talk therapy, which one of the following is recommended as first-line therapy for this condition?
questions.critique: Poststroke depression (PSD) is a common complication that occurs in 20%–60% of patients with stroke, depending on the definition used in diagnosis and the timing of symptom onset. The exact pathophysiology of PSD is unknown but appears complex and multifactorial. The incidence of PSD varies with the location of the stroke. Abnormal neurotransmitter and inflammatory marker levels have been associated with PSD. SSRIs such as escitalopram are considered first-line therapy for PSD. Other options include SNRIs, tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs), and newer multimodal agents such as vortioxetine. Bupropion may be considered if SSRIs are ineffective or cannot be tolerated. As a class, TCAs have a higher anticholinergic effect than SSRIs and are therefore not preferred, although nortriptyline has been evaluated in randomized, controlled trials and found to be effective in treating PSD. There is a growing body of evidence to support the use of repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation, but it is not recommended as first-line treatment at this time.

questions.stem: Patients with which one of the following should be provided opioid overdose education and a prescription for naloxone?

options.A: A history of substance use disorder
options.B: A prescription for alprazolam (Xanax), 1 mg nightly as needed
options.C: A 3-day prescription for hydrocodone/acetaminophen, 5/325 mg twice daily
options.D: A prescription through hospice treatment for morphine, 10 mg as needed

questions.critique: The 2022 CDC clinical practice guideline for prescribing opioids recommends that clinicians offer naloxone to patients at increased risk of overdose, including patients with a history of substance use, overdose, and sleep-disordered breathing, as well as patients who take higher dosages of opioids (≥50 MME/day), take benzodiazepines with opioids, or are at risk of returning to higher opioid dosages with loss of tolerance, such as when tapering opioid dosages.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old female with a medical history of hypothyroidism, hypertension, and morbid obesity with a recent gastric bypass surgery presents with a history of dizziness, sweating, shaking, fatigue, and occasional fainting, occurring 1–2 hours after consuming beverages containing dairy products or a high sugar content. Her blood glucose levels, measured with her husband's glucometer during each episode, range between 50 mg/dL and 55 mg/dL. Which one of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?
questions.critique: The most likely cause of this patient's symptoms is late dumping syndrome. Dumping syndrome is a common postbariatric surgery complication occurring in about 40% of patients. There are 2 types of dumping syndrome: early dumping syndrome and late dumping syndrome. Early dumping syndrome is caused by the rapid passage of nutrients into the small bowel, resulting in intestinal hormonal release and fluid shift. Symptoms occur within 30–60 minutes of eating and include abdominal pain, diarrhea, bloating, nausea, flushing, palpitations, sweating, tachycardia, hypotension, and syncope without chemical evidence of hypoglycemia. Late dumping syndrome is caused by the rapid absorption of carbohydrates, leading to exaggerated insulin secretion, resulting in chemical evidence of hypoglycemia occurring 1–3 hours after eating. Gastroparesis, unlike dumping syndrome, results from significantly delayed gastric emptying without hypoglycemia, often due to complications of diabetes mellitus. Idiopathic postprandial syndrome describes hypoglycemic-like symptoms that occur after eating without chemical evidence of hypoglycemia. Insulinoma is a rare insulin-producing tumor of the pancreas that causes hypoglycemia regardless of food intake timing.

questions.stem: A 10-month-old male is brought to your office with a 1-day history of fussiness and tugging at his ear. The child's mother says that he has had a fever this morning. He is up to date on all immunizations, and he does not have a history of previous ear infections or recent illness. He has a history of hives after being given amoxicillin. On examination the child has a temperature of 39.2 °C (102.6 °F) but seems to be only slightly uncomfortable. When you examine his ears you note moderate bulging of the left tympanic membrane and normal findings in the right ear. All other findings are normal. According to the American Academy of Pediatrics guidelines, which one of the following would be the most appropriate initial management?
questions.critique: The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends antibiotic therapy for children ≥6 months of age with severe signs and symptoms of acute otitis media (AOM), including moderate or severe otalgia, otalgia lasting longer than 48 hours, or a temperature ≥39 °C (102 °F), whether the AOM is unilateral or bilateral (SOR B). Children younger than 24 months without severe symptoms should receive antibiotic therapy for bilateral AOM, whereas older children or those with unilateral AOM can be offered the option of observation and follow-up. The usual treatment for AOM is amoxicillin, but an antibiotic with additional β-lactamase coverage, such as amoxicillin/clavulanate, should be given if the child has received amoxicillin within the past 30 days, has concurrent purulent conjunctivitis, or has a history of AOM unresponsive to amoxicillin (SOR C). Penicillin-allergic patients should be treated with an alternative antibiotic such as cefdinir, cefuroxime, cefpodoxime, or ceftriaxone. Ciprofloxacin should not be used in children. Sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim is no longer effective for the treatment of acute otitis media due to evidence of Streptococcus pneumoniae resistance.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old male is brought to the emergency department (ED) by his parents for evaluation after falling from a kitchen table just prior to arrival. The mother states that the child climbed onto the table and fell off before she could reach him. He landed directly on the right side of his head and did not lose consciousness. She witnessed 1 episode of vomiting after the fall but no additional episodes since then. On physical examination, the patient is tearful but consolable. You note a right-sided parietal hematoma. There are no palpable skull fractures or signs of altered mental status. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Providing reassurance and recommending outpatient follow-up
options.B: Observation in the ED for worsening symptoms
options.D: CT of the head and neck

questions.critique: This patient likely has a mild traumatic brain injury (mTBI), or concussion, based on the mechanism of injury and the presence of vomiting. This patient's TBI would be classified as mild due to the lack of worsening symptoms or additional concerning findings on examination, such as the child being fussier or holding his head. For these reasons, reassurance and outpatient observation with follow-up is the most appropriate next step. Routine imaging is not recommended for mTBI in children. There is no role for radiography of the skull in the management of mTBI or concussion. CT of the head or neck would only be indicated if the patient were experiencing acutely worsening symptoms, reporting or showing signs of headache, or had a suspected head or neck injury. There is no need to contact child protective services because there has been no delay in seeking care or the presence of other unusual physical findings or behaviors between the parents and child.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old male presents for advice on smoking cessation. He has smoked 10 cigarettes per day for the past 10 years. His grandmother was recently diagnosed with lung cancer, and he is motivated to quit. You advise him that smoking cessation is the most important action that he can take for his health, and you counsel him about effective behavioral interventions. Which one of the following behavioral changes would improve this patient's chances of smoking cessation?
questions.critique: Behavioral interventions are effective treatments for smoking cessation. Advising a patient who wishes to stop smoking to create a smoke-free home and car environment is a highly effective behavioral intervention. This advice is an important aspect of a brief 3-step tobacco cessation intervention model (Ask, Advise, Assist/Connect). Caffeine intake does not play a role in smoking cessation. Switching to menthol cigarettes does not have a role in supporting smoking cessation. There is no high- or moderate-quality evidence to support hypnotherapy or acupuncture for smoking cessation. In addition to behavioral interventions, the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force also recommends providing FDA-approved pharmacotherapy such as nicotine replacement therapy, sustained-release bupropion hydrochloride, or varenicline. Combined interventions result in increased tobacco-cessation rates.

questions.stem: Which one of the following medications is the recommended initial management of COPD in patients who are classified as GOLD group A?
questions.critique: Long-acting muscarinic antagonists (LAMAs) such as umeclidinium are recommended as the preferred initial management of COPD that is classified as GOLD group A. Long-acting β-agonists (LABAs) such as formoterol may be used as an alternative for these patients. LAMA/LABA combinations such as aclidinium bromide/formoterol are recommended as initial management of COPD that is classified as GOLD group B. LAMA/LABA combinations are also recommended for management of COPD that is classified as GOLD group E. Inhaled corticosteroid (ICS)/LAMA/LABA combinations such as fluticasone furoate/umeclidinium/vilanterol can also be considered for initial management of COPD that is classified as GOLD group E, especially if blood eosinophil levels are ≥300 cells/μL.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old female with type 2 diabetes is brought to the emergency department by her family, who state that she has grown increasingly confused over the last few days, with additional symptoms of polyuria and polydipsia. Her initial vital signs demonstrate hypotension and tachycardia. Laboratory Findings Serum glucose 750 mg/dL Serum osmolality 350 mOsm/kg H2O (N 275–295) Urine ketones trace Arterial pH 7.3 Anion gap normal Which one of the following would be the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.D: Intravenous isotonic fluid hydration with sodium bicarbonate

questions.critique: This patient presents with acute confusion, dehydration, hyperglycemia, and hyperosmolality. The appropriate diagnosis is hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state (HHS), rather than diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), given her lack of significant ketonuria and acidosis. She is hypovolemic and dehydrated as evidenced by her change in vital signs. Initial treatment consists of intravenous fluids with isotonic solutions, such as 0.9% normal saline or lactated Ringer solution. She will also need an intravenous insulin infusion of short-acting insulin at a dose of 0.05 units/kg/hr. After the initial fluid bolus, potassium chloride can be added to intravenous fluids to correct her total body potassium depletion. Sodium bicarbonate should only be considered if the arterial pH is <7.0 (see Figure 4 of the American Diabetes Association guideline for treatment pathways of DKA and HHS).

questions.stem: A 65-year-old male with symptomatic heart failure presents for follow-up. He currently has no symptoms of dyspnea at rest, but has moderate to significant limitations with ordinary activity. His most recent echocardiogram showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 38%. His last hemoglobin A1c was the highest it has ever been at 6.3%, and he does not take any glucose-lowering medications. His current medications include losartan (Cozaar), carvedilol (Coreg), furosemide (Lasix), and spironolactone (Aldactone). On examination he has a blood pressure of 120/75 mm Hg and an oxygen saturation of 94% on room air, and there are no pulmonary crackles or peripheral edema. Adding which one of the following would be most likely to further reduce this patient’s mortality risk?
questions.critique: Treatment with an SGLT2 inhibitor is recommended to decrease all-cause mortality in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), regardless of the presence or absence of diabetes mellitus. According to guidelines from the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association, first-line medical management for symptomatic HFrEF is angiotensin receptor/neprilysin inhibitor (ARNi) therapy. If ARNi therapy is not feasible, an ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker is second-line therapy. These patients should also receive therapy with a cardioselective β-blocker, a loop diuretic (for pulmonary congestion or edema), and a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist. Neither calcium channel blockers (CCBs) nor thiazide diuretics provide mortality benefits in HFrEF; although thiazide diuretics may be used for the adjunctive management of symptomatic congestion and edema, CCBs may lead to worse cardiac outcomes. The potential mortality benefits of GLP-1 receptor agonists in patients with HFrEF are still being investigated. Metformin has been shown to improve mortality in patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (left ventricular ejection fraction >50%).

questions.stem: A 45-year-old male with a past medical history of hyperlipidemia and obesity presents with concerns of polydipsia and polyuria that have progressively worsened over the last 2 weeks. His older sister was recently diagnosed with type 2 diabetes and your patient asks to be tested. Which one of the following test results would be sufficient to diagnose diabetes in this patient?

options.B: A 1-hour plasma glucose level ≥140 mg/dL on 50-g oral glucose testing
options.C: A fasting plasma glucose level ≥120 mg/dL
options.D: A random blood glucose level ≥200 mg/dL

questions.critique: According to guidelines from the American Diabetes Association, diabetes mellitus can be diagnosed with a random blood glucose level ≥200 mg/dL in patients with classic symptoms of hyperglycemia, such as polyuria and polydipsia, as seen in this patient. An 8- hour fasting plasma glucose level ≥126 mg/dL, a hemoglobin A1c ≥6.5%, or a 2-hour plasma glucose level ≥200 mg/dL on 75-g oral glucose tolerance testing can also indicate diabetes but would require confirmatory testing with a second abnormal laboratory value to confirm the diagnosis. A fasting plasma glucose level 100–125 mg/dL or a hemoglobin A1c 5.7%–6.4% suggests a diagnosis of prediabetes. A 1-hour plasma glucose level ≥140 mg/dL on 50-g oral glucose testing is an abnormal first step in a 2-step screening approach for gestational diabetes mellitus in pregnant individuals.

questions.stem: A 36-year-old male presents to your office with continued pain in his right arm after sustaining a right forearm fracture 6 weeks ago that was treated with cast immobilization. At the time of the injury, he noted a severe, throbbing pain in his right forearm that improved with NSAID therapy. Now, he feels a severe, sharp pain with burning and numbness throughout the arm and hand that worsens with any pressure, including clothing. He reports weakness in his right hand and notes that he drops objects such as cups. He is afebrile and his vital signs are within normal limits. An examination reveals pain to light touch in the right arm. The right arm is warmer to the touch than the left arm with trace edema noted. The patient's right-hand grip is 4/5, and his left-hand grip is 5/5. You note 2+ radial and brachial pulses and normal bilateral reflexes. Which one of the following is the most likely etiology of this patient's pain?
questions.critique: This patient meets the Budapest criteria for complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) including hyperalgesia, temperature asymmetry, evidence of edema, and motor weakness following trauma to the limb. CRPS is a clinical diagnosis. The absence of fever or other signs of infection makes cellulitis unlikely. The examination findings make factitious disorder and functional neurologic symptom disorder (conversion disorder) unlikely. The absence of the characteristic stocking-glove altered sensation makes peripheral neuropathy unlikely.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old nulligravida has a 2- to 3-day history of severe lower abdominal pain. On examination the patient’s temperature is 37.7 °C (99.9 °F), bowel sounds are normal, and there is diffuse lower abdominal tenderness without rebound. On pelvic examination there is adnexal tenderness and pain on cervical motion. Vaginal discharge shows leukocytes. A pregnancy test is negative. In addition to sexually transmitted infection testing and empiric treatment, which one of the following should you recommend?

options.B: A repeat pregnancy test in 7 days

questions.critique: This patient's likely diagnosis is pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), and the diagnosis can be made clinically in most cases. In women at risk of PID, treatment should be initiated if any of the following are present: cervical motion tenderness, uterine tenderness, or adnexal tenderness. These criteria are considered sensitive for the diagnosis, but the clinician should be mindful to evaluate for other causes, such as appendicitis or tubo- ovarian abscess (TOA) as indicated by the history and physical examination. Appendicitis would likely cause unilateral pain. TOA often presents acutely with unilateral pain on examination and an adnexal mass. These findings may prompt the clinician to pursue additional testing such as pelvic ultrasonography. A pelvic ultrasound may show fluid-filled or thickened tubes in the setting of PID, but it is not routinely indicated. MRI may be necessary if an ultrasound were to demonstrate TOA or findings other than simple PID. CT of the abdomen may be completed to evaluate complications above the pelvis. Laparoscopy would not be the initial evaluation. This patient is acutely ill and repeating a pregnancy test a week later would not be appropriate.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old male with a past medical history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia presents to the urgent care clinic approximately 20 hours after experiencing an episode of sudden-onset right-hand weakness. He was suddenly unable to grasp his utensils while eating dinner. These symptoms resolved after about 20 minutes. His wife had encouraged him to seek urgent evaluation, but he declined because his symptoms had resolved. Today the patient's vital signs include a blood pressure of 124/72 mm Hg, a heart rate of 80 beats/min, and a respiratory rate of 12/min. The neurologic examination findings are normal. An EKG shows normal sinus rhythm. The ABCD2 score is 4. Further management includes which one of the following regimens?

options.B: Clopidogrel (Plavix), 300 mg on day 1, followed by 75 mg daily
options.C: Clopidogrel, 300 mg on day 1, followed by 75 mg daily on days 2–10; plus aspirin, 81 mg daily
options.D: Clopidogrel, 300 mg on day 1, followed by 75 mg daily on days 2–21; plus aspirin, 81 mg daily

questions.critique: In patients presenting with a moderate- to high-risk TIA or minor stroke within 24 hours of symptom onset, dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) for 21 days prior to transitioning to a single antiplatelet agent decreases the 90-day stroke risk from 7.8% to 5.2% compared to aspirin alone. This patient has symptoms consistent with a high-risk TIA based on an ABCD2 score (age, blood pressure, clinical features of TIA, duration, and diabetes mellitus history) of 4. Recommended management includes loading doses of clopidogrel (300–600 mg) and aspirin (50–325 mg) within 24 hours of symptom onset followed by clopidogrel (75 mg) and aspirin (50–325 mg daily) for 21 days. The patient can then transition to a single antiplatelet agent. Initial treatment with a single antiplatelet agent or with DAPT for less than 21 days does not reduce stroke risk compared to DAPT for 21 days.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old patient with a previous history of opioid use disorder and untreated hypertension presents to your office to establish care. They are now in recovery and would like to catch up on screening laboratory studies. Their blood pressure today is 160/90 mm Hg. Laboratory studies show elevated AST, ALT, and alkaline phosphatase levels and are positive for hepatitis C antibody and hepatitis C viral load. Testing for hepatitis B and HIV is negative. Lipids are elevated. You calculate an atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease 10-year risk score of 10%. A right upper quadrant ultrasound shows fibrotic changes of the liver. The patient is surprised that they have liver damage, as they feel well and have never consumed much alcohol. In addition to referring the patient to a gastroenterologist, which one of the following should you begin now to improve fibrotic changes?

options.E: Upper endoscopy with banding of any varices

questions.critique: Treatment of hepatitis C in patients with metabolic dysfunction–associated steatohepatitis (formerly known as nonalcoholic steatohepatitis) can improve fibrotic changes, as can bariatric surgery. Prophylactic antibiotics should be initiated to prevent spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) in patients with a history of SBP; low ascitic fluid protein levels (<1.5 g/dL); advanced hepatic disease as indicated by a Child-Pugh score ≥9 or a bilirubin level ≥3 mg/dL; or renal disease as indicated by a serum creatinine level ≥1.2 mg/dL or a serum sodium level ≤130 mEq/L. Nonselective β-blockers should be started in patients with significant varices seen on upper endoscopy. Coinfection with hepatitis C virus and HIV increases a patient's risk of fibrotic progression to cirrhosis. A conversation regarding risks and benefits of initiating pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) would be beneficial; however, PrEP has not been shown to improve fibrotic changes. Offering treatment for viral hepatitis along with PrEP increases access for patients who are at risk of coinfection. In individuals with metabolic dysfunction–associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) (formerly known as nonalcoholic fatty liver disease) and cirrhosis, antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended in specific scenarios: in patients with a history of SBP, during episodes of acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding, and in select high-risk patients with low ascitic fluid protein levels (<1.5 g/dL) along with either advanced liver disease (Child-Pugh score ≥9 and a bilirubin level ≥3 mg/dL) or signs of renal impairment (a creatinine level ≥1.2 mg/dL, a BUN level ≥25 mg/dL, or a sodium level ≤130 mEq/L).

questions.stem: A 46-year-old female sees you for an annual well woman visit. Her only concern is pelvic pressure that becomes more bothersome throughout the day. She notes occasional urinary incontinence of small amounts when coughing or running. She says these symptoms mildly bother her, and mostly wants to make sure there is not a more serious problem. On physical examination, using the lower half of a bivalve speculum, you note a prolapse of the anterior wall, 0.5 cm proximal to the hymenal ring. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step for this patient?
questions.critique: Pelvic organ prolapse is a common problem, and can be described as apical, of the anterior wall, or of the posterior wall. The Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification system can be used to evaluate the prolapse and determine grading. A grade 2 prolapse indicates a prolapse that extends within 1 cm above or below the hymenal ring. Pelvic floor muscle training can reduce prolapse by 1–2 cm and provide subjective improvement in symptoms. There is no clear evidence of the benefit of estrogen alone for pelvic organ prolapse. Surgical management is often the ultimate treatment for pelvic organ prolapse, but it is important to note that urinary incontinence can worsen after repair. This patient is minimally bothered by her symptoms; therefore, surgical treatment would not be recommended at this time but may be indicated in the future. Pelvic organ prolapse is generally progressive until menopause, after which it can alternate between regression and progression.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old male with a past medical history of coronary artery disease, COPD, and hyperlipidemia sees you because of an acute onset of left lower leg pain 1 hour ago. He was in his usual state of health when the pain occurred. His left foot is numb and cold, and he is having difficulty walking. He has a 60-pack-year smoking history. Physical examination findings include a mottled appearance of the left lower leg and foot. Posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis pulses are absent. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate first step in management?

options.D: Expedited outpatient referral to a vascular surgeon
options.E: Transfer to the nearest emergency department with access to a vascular surgeon

questions.critique: This patient's presentation is concerning for acute limb ischemia and he requires immediate transfer to an emergency facility with vascular surgeon accessibility (SOR C). Anticoagulation with unfractionated heparin should be started soon after arrival. Acute limb ischemia is a vascular emergency as skeletal muscle can only survive 4–6 hours without blood flow. Acute limb ischemia is characterized by the 6 P’s: pain, pallor, poikilothermia, pulselessness, paresthesia, and paralysis. Direct-acting oral anticoagulants can be used to manage symptomatic peripheral artery disease (PAD), but not for acute management of limb ischemia. While duplex ultrasonography or CT angiography can be used to determine the presence and location of the obstructed vessel, transfer to an emergency facility should not be delayed for imaging. Referral to a vascular surgeon would delay treatment and would be more appropriate for chronic PAD symptoms.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old male presents with a 5-day history of left lower leg pain over the midshaft of his tibia. Two months ago, he started training for a half marathon. Radiographs performed yesterday at an urgent care clinic were normal. You suspect a bone stress injury. Which one of the following is the preferred definitive imaging modality to evaluate for a bone stress injury?
questions.critique: Bone stress injuries are typically overuse injuries that are caused by an accumulation of bone microdamage. They often occur after an increase in the intensity, frequency, or interval of training. They can also occur when there is structurally compromised bone (osteoporosis, tumors, infections) as normal physiologic loads can damage such compromised bone. Radiography is the initial imaging modality because of its high specificity, despite its low sensitivity. If the radiograph is negative and the patient remains symptomatic, then MRI is the preferred follow-up study for definitive diagnosis. It is preferred over CT and nuclear bone scintigraphy because it has higher sensitivity and specificity and avoids the use of radiation. Repeat radiography is not the preferred definitive imaging modality because radiographs are often negative when taken within the first 2–3 weeks of symptoms. Point-of-care ultrasonography can be used by trained clinicians as an adjunct modality, but its sensitivity is not as high as the sensitivity of MRI.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old female presents to an urgent care clinic with a flare-up of her usually well- controlled chronic urticaria. She takes cetirizine (Zyrtec), 10 mg daily. Which one of the following should you recommend?
questions.critique: The first-line treatment of chronic urticaria is an oral second-generation H1-antihistamine. If that is not effective, a reasonable next step is to increase the H1-antihistamine, up to 4 times the usual FDA-recommended dosing. Current evidence does not strongly support the effectiveness of a pseudoallergen-free diet in managing chronic urticaria. Nasal corticosteroids are effective for allergic rhinitis but have not been found to be useful for chronic urticaria. A short course of oral corticosteroids, such as prednisone, can be effective as a short-term solution for a flare-up but is not recommended for chronic control. Cyclosporine is only recommended for patients with severe refractory symptoms that have not responded to increased dosages of second-generation antihistamines and a trial of H2-blockers. Omalizumab is effective for chronic urticaria and would be indicated if aggressive antihistamine therapy is unsuccessful.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old female comes to your office with an 8-day history of nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, and cough. Based on guidelines from the Infectious Diseases Society of America, this patient should be treated with antibiotic therapy if she has which one of the following additional symptoms?

options.C: An increase in nasal discharge after an initial improvement of symptoms

questions.critique: It is difficult to adequately distinguish between viral sinusitis and acute bacterial rhinosinusitis. However, most instances of acute bacterial rhinosinusitis improve without antibiotics. Major symptoms of sinusitis include purulent nasal discharge, nasal congestion, facial pain or pressure, decreased smell, and fever. Minor symptoms include headaches, cough, fatigue, ear pain or pressure, halitosis, and tooth pain. If adequate follow-up can be ensured, watchful waiting and symptomatic management is recommended for up to 10 days. Patients with severe symptoms (fever ≥39 °C with purulent nasal discharge and/or facial pain) or those with double sickening (new symptoms following improvement of an upper respiratory infection) should be treated immediately with empiric antibiotics.

questions.stem: You are seeing a 44-year-old female who has a 25-pack-year smoking history. She quit smoking 6 years ago after a friend received a diagnosis of lung cancer. According to U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommendations, lung cancer screening should be initiated at what age in this patient?
questions.critique: Lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer death in the United States, with approximately 90% of cases linked to smoking. In 2021, the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommended annual screening with low-dose CT in adults 50–80 years of age who have a 20-pack-year smoking history and either currently smoke or have quit within the past 15 years.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old male presents with a 5-day history of dysuria and mucopurulent penile discharge. On examination the urethral meatus is erythematous and a small amount of discharge is noted. A urine dipstick is positive for leukocyte esterase and a nucleic acid amplification test is negative for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate treatment?

options.D: Gentamicin, 240 mg intramuscularly once, plus azithromycin, 2 g orally once
options.E: Metronidazole, 2 g orally once, plus doxycycline, 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days

questions.critique: This patient has nongonococcal urethritis, which should be treated with azithromycin. Symptoms of urethritis include dysuria, purulent or mucopurulent urethral discharge, and urethral discomfort and/or erythema. Diagnostic criteria include typical signs and symptoms or a history of exposure along with ≥10 WBCs/hpf on a first-void urinalysis, ≥2 WBCs/oil immersion field found on Gram stain of the discharge, or a positive leukocyte esterase with first-void urine. At least half of nongonococcal urethritis cases have no known etiologic agent. Of the cases with known etiologies, Mycoplasma genitalium is responsible for 15%–20%. Treatment options for M genitalium include azithromycin, 1 g orally; doxycycline, 100 mg twice daily for 1 week; or moxifloxacin, 400 mg orally for 7–14 days. If the results of nucleic acid amplification testing for gonorrhea and chlamydia are unavailable, then intramuscular ceftriaxone plus a 7-day course of oral doxycycline would be appropriate and can be used for empiric therapy while awaiting results. Intramuscular gentamicin plus oral azithromycin is used as an alternative treatment regimen for Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Metronidazole is used to treat Trichomonas, which represents 1%– 8% of cases in men and should be diagnosed using a molecular-based test. Levofloxacin is a treatment option for chlamydia and is not first line.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old female presents to the emergency department with what she describes as the worst headache of her life that began suddenly 1 hour ago. Her medical history is significant for tobacco use and uncontrolled hypertension. She indicates that she has had a few headaches over the last month, but today's headache came on suddenly and is extremely intense, unlike the previous headaches. There is no history of head trauma. Her accompanying relative adds that the patient has been experiencing waxing and waning levels of consciousness since the sudden onset of the headache. She has vomited several times, secondary to pain from the headache. Which one of the following is the most important, immediate next step in management?
questions.critique: This patient's clinical picture is most consistent with a subarachnoid hemorrhage. Patients typically describe this as a sudden onset of the worst headache they have experienced. The most immediate and meaningful next step is to order a CT scan of the head to verify the diagnosis of subarachnoid hemorrhage. The diagnosis and management of subarachnoid hemorrhage is time sensitive. Neurosurgery should be consulted immediately if subarachnoid hemorrhage is verified. Brain MRI would likely take longer to obtain than head CT and is not diagnostically advantageous in subarachnoid hemorrhage compared to a head CT. Pain and nausea control are reasonable, but a CT scan of the head should take precedence because of the time sensitivity of diagnosis and management. A lumbar puncture should be considered if the head CT scan is negative but clinical suspicion for subarachnoid hemorrhage remains high, especially if the presentation occurs >6 hours after onset.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old male who uses cannabis daily presents with a 2-week history of persistent nausea and vomiting. He notes little relief from previously prescribed ondansetron but does feel better during a hot shower. While tapering his cannabis use, which one of the following would be most likely to improve this patient's nausea?
questions.critique: This patient has cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome, characterized by heavy chronic cannabis use and nausea that improves with hot baths or showers. Current evidence suggests that haloperidol is more effective than more traditionally used antiemetics, such as metoclopramide or prochlorperazine, or corticosteroids in managing nausea associated with this syndrome. Topical capsaicin has also been shown to be effective. Topical capsaicin, when applied to the upper abdomen, may help alleviate symptoms by activating transient receptor potential vanilloid 1 (TRPV1) receptors. This mechanism can lead to desensitization of sensory nerve fibers, potentially reducing the need for antiemetic medications. Benzodiazepines may help acutely, and tricyclic antidepressants may have efficacy for longer-term management. Cessation of cannabis use is necessary for definitive treatment. Diclofenac gel is not appropriate pharmacotherapy for cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old female with fibromyalgia sees you for follow-up. Her symptoms include widespread pain and prominent fatigue. At your advice, she has engaged in a trial of multidisciplinary, nonpharmacologic treatment including structured exercise, cognitive behavioral therapy, and therapeutic massage, and has experienced only partial improvement in her symptoms. She expresses interest in trying a medication to treat her pain. Which one of the following medications would be most likely to improve this patient's pain?
questions.critique: Duloxetine is an SNRI that is effective for treating fibromyalgia pain (SOR A). Other medications with level A evidence of effectiveness in reducing fibromyalgia-related pain include amitriptyline, cyclobenzaprine, and pregabalin. The SNRI milnacipran also has level A evidence for reducing fibromyalgia-related pain and has evidence for reducing fatigue and improving cognition in patients with fibromyalgia. Other SNRIs, including venlafaxine, have not been studied as extensively. Current evidence is insufficient to support acetaminophen or gabapentin use to treat fibromyalgia pain. Similarly, there is insufficient evidence to support the routine use of opioids such as hydrocodone, which are associated with a significant risk of adverse effects, dependence, misuse, and worsening hyperalgesia. NSAIDs such as ibuprofen are no better than placebo and introduce significant risk of adverse effects.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old male is brought to your office by his parents because of concerns about ongoing nocturnal enuresis. He wets his bed several nights per week despite behavioral interventions. He has no daytime urinary symptoms. Physical examination findings and urinalysis are normal. You discuss treatment options and the parents indicate a preference for medication, as the patient will be attending an overnight summer camp in several weeks. Which one of the following would be the preferred pharmacologic treatment for this child’s nocturnal enuresis?
questions.critique: Behavioral methods and enuresis alarms are first-line treatments for monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis without an identifiable cause. If pharmacotherapy is initiated, intranasal or oral desmopressin is acceptable. Imipramine and oxybutynin are additional options if there is no response to oral desmopressin. Tamsulosin is not recommended for this indication.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old male with a childhood diagnosis of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) presents for re-evaluation of ADHD. He did not take medication for ADHD as a child. He is now having problems with attention and concentration that are affecting his work. He was recently placed on a stimulant medication, but had to discontinue it when his blood pressure and heart rate became elevated. He is still interested in pharmacologic therapy. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next medication?
questions.critique: Stimulants are first-line pharmacotherapy for adults with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) given the extensive data supporting their effectiveness. However, there are potential adverse effects that can prevent patients from taking them, such as headaches, insomnia, poor appetite, weight loss, anxiety, depression, elevated heart rate, and elevated blood pressure. When stimulants are not tolerated or do not provide an adequate response, the second-line therapy would be an SNRI such as atomoxetine or viloxazine. Clonidine and guanfacine are only approved for the treatment of ADHD in children and adolescents. Although they are sometimes used off-label in adults, there is limited evidence to support such use. Tricyclic antidepressants such as nortriptyline have limited evidence and are not approved for ADHD treatment. Escitalopram is not effective for treating ADHD.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old college basketball player consults you for follow-up after sustaining an anterior shoulder dislocation for the first time. He initially presented to the emergency department where his shoulder was reduced and immobilized in a sling. Which one of the following is most likely to decrease the risk of future recurrence?

options.B: Completion of a course of physical therapy
options.C: Administration of an intra-articular corticosteroid injection

questions.critique: Although anterior shoulder dislocations have a high rate of recurrence (up to 90%), evidence from cohort studies has consistently found a lower rate of recurrence in active patients <25 years of age who have received surgical treatment for first-time dislocations. Because of this patient's age and activity level, referral for surgical repair would be appropriate. Other treatment options for anterior shoulder dislocation include the use of a sling for 2–4 weeks with graduated rehabilitation of passive and active range of motion. If no surgery is warranted, athletes can return to play when they are pain free with symmetrical shoulder range of motion and able to perform sport-specific motions, which can be as early as 2–3 weeks after injury. Intra-articular corticosteroids are not advised after a shoulder dislocation.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old gravida 2 para 2 female who is 3 weeks post partum presents to the local urgent care center for left breast pain, swelling, and redness that started a few hours ago. She does not have fever or chills. A breast examination is significant for warm, tender, and mildly erythematous nodules localized to the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. In addition to initiating NSAIDs, which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Applying cold compresses to the affected breast
options.B: Applying warm compresses to the affected breast
options.C: Using a manual breast pump on the affected breast for complete breast emptying
options.D: Obtaining a CBC and C-reactive protein levels

questions.critique: Mastitis is a spectrum of inflammatory breast conditions, which includes lactational or inflammatory mastitis, bacterial mastitis, breast abscess, postpartum breast engorgement, and more. Patients with inflammatory mastitis exhibit unilateral breast erythema, swelling, and pain and may experience systemic inflammatory responses such as fever, malaise, or headache. The initial steps in managing inflammatory mastitis are supportive care with NSAIDs, cold compresses, and watchful waiting. Inflammatory mastitis is diagnosed clinically and does not require laboratory testing or imaging. If a patient's symptoms worsen or do not improve within 24–48 hours with conservative measures, narrow-spectrum antibiotics targeting the most common organisms (Staphylococcus species and Streptococcus species) should be empirically initiated for bacterial mastitis. Warm compresses and frequent breast pumping for complete breast emptying to avoid milk stasis are no longer recommended. CBC and C-reactive protein testing are not helpful as these levels can be elevated in both inflammatory and bacterial mastitis. Breast milk cultures are seldom necessary because they may produce false- positive results caused by normal skin bacteria colonization.

questions.stem: A young couple in your practice presents for preconception counseling. Both partners are known carriers of cystic fibrosis (CF). They ask what risk this poses to their future children. Which one of the following accurately reflects the risk of CF disease transmission to any potential offspring of this couple?

options.A: 25% risk of being affected and 25% risk of being a carrier
options.B: 25% risk of being affected and 50% risk of being a carrier
options.C: 50% risk of being affected and 25% risk of being a carrier
options.D: 50% risk of being affected and 50% risk of being a carrier

questions.critique: Cystic fibrosis is a genetic disorder with an autosomal recessive pattern of inheritance. People who are affected by the disease are homozygous for the condition, with 2 copies of the mutated gene. Carriers are heterozygous for the condition with 1 mutated gene and 1 normal gene. When both partners in a couple are carriers, any potential offspring has a 25% chance of being affected, a 50% chance of being an unaffected carrier, and a 25% chance of being an unaffected noncarrier.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old nurse presents with a 9-month history of fatigue and brain fog that has kept them from working. Their symptoms began after a confirmed diagnosis of COVID-19. They report sleeping at least 7 hours per night but wake unrefreshed and experience lightheadedness throughout the day. They used to run 4 miles 5 days per week but now report feeling exhausted and experience energy crashes after showering and getting dressed. The patient's vital signs and BMI are normal. A physical examination is unremarkable. Their Patient Health Questionnaire–9 (PHQ-9) score is 3. A CBC, TSH level, basic metabolic panel, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate are within the normal range. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.critique: Based on the National Academy of Medicine criteria for myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS), this patient has demonstrated the core symptoms of ME/CFS (unrefreshing sleep, new-onset fatigue, and postexertional malaise) for at least 6 months, in addition to brain fog and lightheadedness.
