Notably, their symptoms developed after a previous COVID-19 infection, an illness that is commonly associated with ME/CFS. A Patient Health Questionnaire–9 (PHQ-9) score of 3 indicates no or minimal depression, which would not account for severe fatigue or energy crashes after minimal exertion. Exercise may actually improve depression. Secondary causes of fatigue, such as fibromyalgia and systemic lupus erythematosus, typically improve with exercise. Although obstructive sleep apnea may cause unrefreshing sleep, it is not associated with profound fatigue and postexertional malaise.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old male is brought to the clinic by his wife for fatigue. His wife says he has been sleeping in the recliner rather than in bed and has been less active over the last several weeks. He reports that his clothes have been fitting tightly and he has gained weight despite feeling full early in a meal. He also notes difficulty bending over to tie his shoes and pet the dog. He has no chest pain or pressure and has not traveled recently. His past medical history is notable for a myocardial infarction 5 years ago treated with a stent, and elevated cholesterol. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history but quit 20 years ago. His medications include aspirin, 81 mg daily; lisinopril (Zestril), 20 mg daily; and metoprolol succinate (Toprol-XL), 50 mg daily. On examination he has a blood pressure of 110/65 mm Hg, a pulse rate of 89 beats/min, and an oxygen saturation of 95% on room air. You also note mildly increased work of breathing, a regular heart rate, and a laterally displaced apical impulse. Which one of the following tests would most likely confirm this patient’s underlying diagnosis?
questions.critique: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) is a major public health concern affecting more than 6.5 million U.S. adults. Family physicians should be familiar with its typical clinical presentations and diagnosis, which is best established using echocardiography. Symptoms vary and may include dyspnea, ankle edema, and fatigue. Orthopnea, as evident by modification of sleeping from supine to reclined or semi- reclined, is also common. Symptoms of right-sided heart failure, such as early satiety, abdominal bloating, ill-fitting clothes, and shortness of breath when bent forward, are also frequently encountered, but are less specific. Physical examination may reveal signs of congestion such as elevated jugular venous pressure, S3 gallop, or a laterally displaced apical impulse. Some predictors of HFrEF include increased heart rate, coronary artery disease (hazard ratio [HR] 1.73), and previous myocardial infarction (MI) (HR 3.49). The diagnosis is made by documenting left ventricular systolic dysfunction with a left ventricular ejection fraction ≤40% and an elevated BNP level. An EKG is a key component of the workup and evaluation of contributing factors, such as arrhythmia or acute MI, but not diagnosis. A chest x-ray, while quick and readily available, may identify cardiomegaly or vascular congestion but is normal in approximately 20% of those with acute HFrEF, limiting diagnostic utility. Given this patient's history of MI, CT angiography would be inappropriate and is typically reserved for evaluating coronary artery disease in low-risk patients. CT angiography is used to evaluate for pulmonary embolism, which is less likely, given this patient's lack of immobility, prolonged travel, or other risk factors, as well as a history and physical examination that favor heart failure (displaced apical impulse, orthopnea, abdominal distention/weight gain).

questions.stem: A 3-year-old male is brought to your office by his parents for a routine well child visit. He is up to date on vaccines and the parents have no concerns to discuss today. According to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force, this child should be screened for which one of the following conditions?
questions.critique: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) recommends vision screening at least once in all children 3–5 years of age to detect amblyopia or its risk factors (B recommendation). Screening may be performed using visual acuity testing with picture identification, Snellen or "tumbling E," or by an automated device that assesses visual acuity. Early identification of vision disorders can help prevent permanent vision loss due to amblyopia. Anemia screening is recommended for all children at 1 year of age by the American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP), but this approach was found to have insufficient evidence of benefit by the USPSTF (I recommendation). Screening for autism spectrum disorder is recommended by the AAP at 18 and 24 months of age, but the USPSTF found insufficient evidence that universal screening results in improved care in families without concerns about their child's development (I recommendation). The USPSTF found insufficient evidence of benefit of screening for dental caries in children <5 years of age (I recommendation). Instead, it recommends that fluoride varnish be applied every 3–6 months in all children 6 months to 5 years of age to prevent dental caries (B recommendation). This should be performed in the family physician's office if it has not been applied by a dentist. Additionally, an oral fluoride supplement is recommended to prevent dental caries in children without adequate fluoride in their water supply (B recommendation). Universal hearing screening is recommended for newborns, but screening at this age is not recommended.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old female comes to your clinic for an annual wellness examination. Her past medical history is notable for dermatomyositis diagnosed 2 years ago. She is otherwise healthy and her BMI is normal. Patients with dermatomyositis are at increased risk of which one of the following conditions?
questions.critique: While counseling on diet and exercise and screening for diabetes mellitus and hyperlipidemia are important, malignancy screening is the most important component of the wellness visit in this patient with dermatomyositis diagnosed after the age of 40. Cancer is a leading cause of death in this patient population and is diagnosed in up to 25% of these patients within 3 years of their dermatomyositis diagnosis. The most likely cancers in this population include adenocarcinomas of the ovary, lung, pancreas, stomach, and colon as well as non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Population-level cancer screening recommendations are generally insufficient for this population, and advanced cancer screening modalities, including CT scans and PET scans, may be recommended based on the patient's individual risk factors to target these types of cancers. Chronic kidney disease and obesity have not been observed to have an increased risk in patients with dermatomyositis.

questions.stem: Of the following cognitive biases, which one best describes the practice of prioritizing data that supports an initial impression, even when the initial impression is incorrect?
questions.critique: Anchoring bias describes the practice of physicians prioritizing information and data that supports their initial impressions, even when these impressions are incorrect. In other words, it is when physicians are biased toward their first impression. Availability bias describes the likelihood of a diagnosis being erroneously linked to how easy it is to think of examples. Implicit bias is defined as attitudes and beliefs about race, ethnicity, age, ability, gender, or other characteristics. Implicit bias operates on an unconscious level and can only be measured indirectly. Outcomes bias refers to the attribution of all results to prior decisions, regardless of whether there is a reason to do so.

questions.stem: An otherwise healthy 15-year-old female presents for evaluation of a 7-month history of heavy menstrual bleeding. Her periods are 10 days long, irregular, and require 10 tampons per day. She sometimes passes large clots. Other than some fatigue, she feels well today and her vital signs are normal. A point-of-care hemoglobin level is 10.2 g/dL (N 12.0–16.0). Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
questions.critique: Heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) is very common in adolescents and may be a sign of an underlying bleeding disorder. Although bleeding disorders affect 1%–2% of the general population, the prevalence increases to 20% among adolescent girls with heavy menstrual bleeding. Platelet dysfunction and von Willebrand disease are most commonly identified. Evaluation for bleeding disorders is indicated when there is frequent pad or tampon use (>1 every 1–2 hours); menstrual bleeding lasting longer than 7 days; passage of clots larger than 1 inch in diameter; a personal history of anemia treatment, excessive bleeding with tooth extraction or surgery, or excessive bruising; or a family history of a bleeding disorder. The initial workup for HMB in an adolescent includes a CBC; a prothrombin time, partial thromboplastin time, and INR; a von Willebrand panel; and hCG, ferritin, fibrinogen, and TSH levels. Testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea should be considered in sexually active patients. Testosterone, prolactin, and DHEA levels should be obtained if there are signs of polycystic ovary syndrome or other endocrine disorders. Levels of FSH, LH, estrogen, and progesterone are not helpful in the evaluation of excessive uterine bleeding. Pelvic imaging is not routinely necessary but should be considered for HMB that does not respond to initial management. Hormonal contraceptives and tranexamic acid may be used for the treatment of HMB, but workup for a bleeding disorder is the most important first step.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old patient presents with a 6-month history of epigastric burning pain that occurs several days per week. The pain does not seem to have a clear correlation to eating. There has been no substernal pain or regurgitation and no weight loss, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, fever, night sweats, or fatigue. The patient has no prior medical history and takes no medications, but has tried calcium carbonate occasionally without benefit. A physical examination reveals normal vital signs and no abdominal abnormalities or enlarged lymph nodes. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step?

options.B: Stool antigen testing for Helicobacter pylori

questions.critique: This patient presents with symptoms of dyspepsia. Dyspepsia is a diagnosis of exclusion and should be considered if at least 1 of the following symptoms is present: early satiety, epigastric pain, epigastric burning, and postprandial fullness. The majority of patients diagnosed with dyspepsia have functional dyspepsia with no evidence of organic disease on endoscopy. Testing for and treating Helicobacter pylori infection is recommended in patients with dyspepsia, with evidence of improvement 1 year after treatment. The stool antigen test and the urea breath test are the preferred noninvasive options for detecting H pylori. Serum testing for H pylori is not recommended because it has lower sensitivity and specificity than other noninvasive tests. Sucralfate has not been shown to be effective for dyspepsia. Abdominal ultrasonography, particularly of the upper abdomen, is indicated when evaluating for liver pathology or gallbladder disease but is not useful for investigating dyspepsia. Endoscopy is recommended for patients >60 years of age with new dyspepsia, and for patients <60 years of age with multiple or severe alarm symptoms, such as marked weight loss, rapidly progressive dysphagia, an epigastric mass, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old female comes to your office for a health maintenance examination. She is currently asymptomatic but has a medical history of renal calculi. Laboratory studies reveal a serum calcium level of 12.1 mg/dL (N 8.7–10.2), confirmed by an ionized calcium level. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step at this time?

options.D: CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis

questions.critique: Hypercalcemia is commonly identified through a metabolic panel and should be confirmed with a corrected or ionized calcium level. The most common cause of hypercalcemia is primary hyperparathyroidism, but any underlying malignancy should be ruled out. A thorough history should assess for symptoms such as arrhythmias, hypertension, abdominal pain, arthralgias, fractures, mental status changes, anxiety, depression, and nephrolithiasis. Dietary intake of calcium and phosphorus, use of medications such as lithium, and a family history of multiple endocrine neoplasia are also important to evaluate. After confirming hypercalcemia, the initial test should be a parathyroid hormone (PTH) level. If the PTH level is elevated, then 25-hydroxyvitamin D, creatinine, and 24-hour urinary calcium levels should be measured in addition to ordering a bone density test. Imaging of the parathyroid glands is not necessary for diagnosis, but is useful if surgical management is indicated. A low PTH level could suggest malignancy and may indicate the need for a CT scan.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old female presents to your office with a 3-day history of right-sided facial weakness. She has flattening of the nasolabial fold and is unable to elevate the right corner of her mouth. She is able to close her right eye and raise her eyebrows. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.critique: This patient presents with a relatively acute onset of facial weakness affecting only the right side of the face, but she is able to close her right eye and raise her eyebrows. Sparing of the forehead muscles is consistent with a supranuclear lesion because the nucleus of cranial nerve VII receives fibers from both sides of the cortex for the upper face, meaning the forehead would be unaffected. Cranial nerve VII includes visceral efferent fibers (facial muscles and stapedius), visceral motor fibers (salivary and lacrimal glands), and special sensory fibers (taste to the anterior two-thirds of the tongue). In Bell palsy, which involves edema of this cranial nerve, the entire side of the face is affected, including the forehead. Therefore, this presentation is not consistent with Bell palsy and warrants further evaluation, including brain MRI, to assess for a central cause. Prednisone and valacyclovir are not appropriate for this patient, as they are treatments used for Bell palsy. Plasmapheresis is not indicated for Bell palsy, stroke, or tumor. Surgical decompression is not indicated.

questions.stem: On examination she is neurovascularly intact. Her vital signs are normal except for an elevated heart rate of 110 beats/min and a blood pressure of 150/92 mm Hg. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient's injury?

options.B: Observation in the ED, and acetaminophen
options.C: Observation in the ED, and ibuprofen

questions.critique: This patient likely has an upper gastrointestinal (GI) bleed from erosive gastritis or peptic ulcer disease. This is suggested by a history of abdominal pain and nausea and the consistent use of ibuprofen with alcohol. The patient's BUN is elevated, and his hemoglobin level shows anemia. Given that the patient is hemodynamically stable and his hemoglobin level is above the transfusion threshold, he does not need excessive workup prior to treatment, but guidelines recommend initiating a proton pump inhibitor on presentation with no delay regardless of status for endoscopy. Urgent endoscopy is only indicated in patients with hemodynamic instability and signs of upper GI bleeding. There are no randomized, controlled trials or guidelines to support the use of nasogastric lavage. Current guidelines recommend blood transfusion for patients with upper gastrointestinal bleeding when the hemoglobin level is <7 g/dL in most cases or <8 g/dL (80 g/L) in patients with coronary artery disease, recent cardiac surgery, or hematologic malignancies. Arterial embolization is only performed if endoscopy has failed to control bleeding.

questions.stem: A 3-day-old infant presents to your clinic for a routine check-up. The parents report that the infant has been feeding well but has had fewer wet diapers than expected. On examination, the infant appears slightly lethargic with dry skin. You need to assess the hydration status of the infant. Which one of the following would be the most reliable clinical sign for assessing dehydration in this 3-day-old infant?
questions.critique: Capillary refill time is the most reliable clinical sign for assessing dehydration in infants. An abnormal capillary refill time (>2 seconds) is a strong indicator of dehydration and has been shown to have a high likelihood ratio for predicting dehydration in children. While decreased urine output is a sensitive indicator of dehydration, it is not specific and can be influenced by other factors such as feeding patterns and ambient temperature. Dry mucous membranes are a useful sign but can be subjective and less reliable compared to capillary refill time. Abnormal skin turgor is more commonly used in older children and is less reliable in neonates due to their higher skin elasticity. A sunken fontanelle can indicate dehydration, but it is less specific and can be influenced by other factors, such as the patient's position during the examination.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old female presents with her parents because she has been experiencing a loss of interest in her usual activities, trouble sleeping, difficulty concentrating, and low energy over the last month. After a discussion with the patient and her parents, you refer her for cognitive behavioral therapy and start an antidepressant. Which one of the following medications is approved by the FDA to treat depression in patients such as this?
questions.critique: Two SSRIs, fluoxetine and escitalopram, have been approved by the FDA for the treatment of depression in adolescents. Other SSRIs such as sertraline, NDRIs such as bupropion, and SNRIs such as venlafaxine are often used off-label for this purpose but have not been approved by the FDA for use in adolescents. Esketamine is a ketamine nasal spray used in conjunction with an oral antidepressant for treatment-resistant depression in adults.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old patient presents with a 14-day history of a productive cough and green sputum. They report they initially had a fever but that it resolved after a few days. The cough is interfering with their sleep and daily activity, and they have tried cough drops without relief. They have a history of hypothyroidism for which they take levothyroxine (Synthroid), and do not have a history of asthma. They do not smoke, and their immunizations are up to date. On examination, the patient is afebrile and has an oxygen saturation of 98% on room air, a heart rate of 82 beats/min, and a blood pressure of 120/82 mm Hg. A lung examination reveals mild rhonchi that clear with cough, and you do not note any wheezing or egophony. Which one of the following treatments would be the most effective in reducing this patient's cough?
questions.critique: Acute bronchitis is a lower respiratory tract infection that is most commonly caused by a virus. Treatment is generally supportive as it is a self-limited illness. Guaifenesin, honey, and dextromethorphan have been shown to decrease the frequency of cough (SOR B). In otherwise healthy patients without evidence of pneumonia, acute bronchitis can last 2–3 weeks. Patients may exhibit purulent sputum, rhonchi, and wheezing with acute bronchitis but are typically afebrile with normal oxygen saturation. β2-Agonists such as albuterol are not recommended for the treatment of acute bronchitis unless wheezing is present (SOR B). While 90% of acute bronchitis cases are caused by viruses, a significant percentage are treated inappropriately with antibiotics. Antibiotics can decrease the duration of cough by 0.46 days, but the overall net benefit of antibiotics is negligible and often outweighed by potential side effects (SOR A). Studies show codeine has no benefit in the treatment of acute bronchitis and has rather significant side effects.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old male presents to the urgent care center for left ankle pain and swelling following an ankle inversion injury that occurred a few hours earlier. A physical examination reveals mild swelling of the left ankle joint. Using the Ottawa foot and ankle rules, which one of the following findings, if present, would be an indication for radiography for an acute ankle inversion injury?

options.A: Bone tenderness at the anterior edge of the lateral malleolus
options.B: Bone tenderness at the anterior edge of the medial malleolus
options.C: Bone tenderness at the base of the fourth metatarsal
options.D: Bone tenderness of the navicular bone
options.E: Inability to bear weight on the left foot for at least 10 steps after the injury

questions.critique: When a foot or ankle fracture is suspected, a weight-bearing foot and ankle x-ray should be obtained. The Ottawa foot and ankle rules are a useful tool for determining the need for an x-ray for an acute ankle inversion injury. According to the Ottawa foot and ankle rules, a foot x-ray should be obtained if any of the following requirements are met: bone tenderness at the posterior edge or the tip of the lateral or medial malleolus, bone tenderness at the base of the fifth metatarsal or navicular bone, or inability to bear weight on the affected leg or foot for at least 4 steps after the injury.

questions.stem: A generally healthy 13-year-old male presents with his parents because they are concerned that he has very large-volume stools that clog the toilet. The patient says that he averages fewer than 2 bowel movements per week and they are sometimes painful. He does not have any abdominal pain and has not seen any blood in his stools. Examination findings are unremarkable. His height, weight, and BMI percentiles have remained steady for years. A CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, ferritin level, and TSH level are all normal. An abdominal x-ray is normal. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step?
questions.critique: This patient meets 3 of the Rome IV diagnostic criteria for functional constipation (≤2 bowel movements per week, painful hard stools, and stools that clog the toilet). There is good evidence that polyethylene glycol is effective for treating functional constipation in children and adolescents. There is no evidence that probiotics are effective. This patient does not have any red flag symptoms that would prompt a need for either magnetic resonance enterography to evaluate for inflammatory bowel disease or a colonoscopy to evaluate for a structural lesion.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old male sees you for routine follow-up of diabetes mellitus. He smokes 1 pack of cigarettes per day and does not have any other medical conditions. His diabetes is well controlled on liraglutide (Victoza) and metformin. He has an LDL-cholesterol level of 120 mg/dL, an HDL-cholesterol level of 55 mg/dL, and a triglyceride level of 300 mg/dL. His 10-year atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease risk is 8.2%. According to 2018 guidelines from the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association, the most appropriate recommendation for this patient would be
questions.critique: Patients 40–75 years of age with diabetes mellitus and an LDL-cholesterol level ≥70 mg/dL should be treated with a moderate-intensity statin without calculating a 10-year risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. However, high-intensity statin therapy should be considered to lower the LDL-cholesterol level by at least 50% for patients such as this one who are at elevated risk, especially those 50–75 years of age or with multiple risk factors. Of the options listed, atorvastatin, 40–80 mg daily, is the only therapy that is considered high-intensity statin therapy. Pravastatin, 20 mg daily, is low-intensity statin therapy. Simvastatin, 20–40 mg daily, is considered moderate-intensity therapy and is not recommended at doses >40 mg daily. Ezetimibe and fenofibrate are not recommended first-line medications for the treatment of hyperlipidemia and the prevention of cardiovascular disease.

questions.stem: A 37-year-old male comes to the primary care clinic with a several-month history of worsening paresthesia in his right foot. He says that he sometimes has numbness and a hot sensation in his feet and has difficulty using the pedals of his car. Sometimes, he has pain that wakes him up at night but eases when he walks around the room. He has no significant past medical history but did sustain a severe right ankle sprain about 1 year ago. His vital signs are unremarkable. Examination of the ankle reveals pain along the medial malleolus and a positive Tinel sign at the ankle. An x-ray of the ankle is unremarkable. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.critique: Tarsal tunnel syndrome is an entrapment neuropathy of the foot caused by compression of the tibial nerve or one of its branches in the fibro-osseous tunnel under the flexor retinaculum and within the abductor hallucis muscle of the foot. It may occur following an injury, such as a fracture or sprain of the ankle. Patients can present with paresthesias, local pain behind the medial malleolus, and loss of sensation in the sole. Paresis of the small muscle of the foot can lead to impaired abduction. There is no criterion standard for the diagnosis of tarsal tunnel syndrome. The most helpful clinical diagnostic sign is a positive Tinel sign at the ankle, and electrophysiologic studies can help in making the diagnosis. MRI can be useful in cases of compression due to a mass lesion. Patients can begin a trial of conservative management, but advanced cases are better treated surgically. Achilles tendinopathy causes posterior heel pain that is localized at the site of insertion of the Achilles tendon and worsens with increased activity or pressure on the area. Haglund deformity presents with pain in the upper part of the posterior calcaneus and is more common in middle-aged women who repeatedly wear ill-fitting shoes. X-rays demonstrate the deformity on the superior aspect of the posterior calcaneus. Patients with heel pad syndrome report a deep-seated pain that feels like a bruise in the middle of the heel. The pain can be reproduced by palpating the area. It is typically an inflammatory response to damage of the heel pad secondary to walking barefoot for prolonged periods of time. Plantar fasciitis causes tenderness along the medial calcaneal tuberosity and along the plantar fascia. Patients typically report pain with their first steps after getting out of bed in the morning.

questions.stem: A 36-year-old female comes to your office for a health maintenance examination. She mentions that she engages in amateur rifle target shooting matches. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate advice concerning this patient’s physical safety?

options.A: She should wear electronic hearing protection that amplifies quiet sounds
options.D: She should wear her regular glasses so that she has eye protection

questions.critique: Hearing protection is essential while shooting firearms because noise exposure often exceeds 140 dB. Even a single gunshot can cause permanent hearing loss. Earmuffs and earplugs with a noise reduction rate of 28–31 dB are common and inexpensive protective devices. Electronic hearing protection can amplify quiet sounds such as a range safety officer's instructions, but also safely protect against loud sounds. Many ranges require their use. Noise-canceling headphones do not provide enough noise reduction for gunshots. Wearing loose-fitting clothing can trap the ejected hot brass casings, leading to unsafe gun handling. Safety eyeglasses that meet the necessary certifications can protect against shrapnel from the bullet striking the target or a malfunction. Regular glasses do not provide sufficient eye protection.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old male presents to your office with a 2-hour history of discomfort in the midsternal area that began while taking a walk earlier today. He says that he feels 90% better. He had a Welcome to Medicare visit 1 year ago, in which an EKG was normal. You order an EKG today. Which one of the following EKG findings, if present in this patient, should prompt urgent transport to the emergency department?

options.E: Voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy

questions.critique: Approximately 1% of primary care office visits are for evaluation of chest pain, in which 2%–4% of these patients have unstable angina or myocardial infarction. Twelve-lead EKG is recommended to look for ST-segment changes, new-onset left bundle branch block, presence of Q waves, and new T-wave inversions. The presence of new-onset left bundle branch block increases the likelihood of acute coronary syndrome (ACS) and acute myocardial infarction. Patients with suspected ACS or the aforementioned changes on EKG should be transported immediately to the emergency department. The presence of first-degree atrioventricular block, frequent PVCs, sinus tachycardia, or voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy does not increase the likelihood of ACS.

questions.stem: A generally healthy 56-year-old female sees you for follow-up of pneumonia diagnosed in the emergency department (ED). A chest x-ray performed in the ED showed a left lower lobe infiltrate and several compression fractures involving the thoracic vertebrae. A DXA scan performed 7 months ago showed a T-score of –1.9. Her daily medications include a multivitamin; calcium, 1000 mg; and vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol), 800 IU. Today she feels well and has no concerns. This patient would be most likely to benefit from therapy for which one of the following conditions?
questions.critique: Although this patient's T-score indicates osteopenia, the presence of fragility fractures indicates osteoporosis. The T-score is obtained by measuring bone mineral density using DXA at the spine, total hip, or femoral neck. A T-score between –1.0 and –2.49 indicates osteopenia, whereas a T-score of ≤–2.5 denotes osteoporosis. However, postmenopausal osteoporosis may be diagnosed by bone mineral density or by the presence of a fragility fracture, which is a fracture that occurs with no associated trauma or after a fall from standing height or lower. Hence, osteoporosis can be diagnosed and the patient further evaluated and treated even though her T-score is >–2.5. Spondylolisthesis is the acquired anterior displacement of one vertebral body over another and is typically associated with degenerative changes. Spondylolysis is a cause of acute back pain resulting from a stress fracture involving the pars interarticularis of the lumbar vertebral body. It is typically found in young athletes.

questions.stem: A gravida 2 para 1 at 28 weeks' gestation presents with an episode of second-trimester bleeding that spontaneously resolved. She has not had any prenatal laboratory studies during either of her pregnancies. Ultrasonography today shows a healthy fetus and a small placental abruption. Which one of the following would be most important to order at this time?

options.E: A urine toxicology screen for cocaine

questions.critique: Blood type/Rh factor testing should be ordered when evaluating second-trimester bleeding. This patient would require a dose of Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM) if found to be Rh negative. Therefore, of all the tests listed, an Rh factor test is the most time sensitive and affects the plan of care. Patients may have had unattended home births with prior pregnancies or refused laboratory tests altogether. With the potential exposure to mixing of maternal and fetal blood during the patient's first delivery, it is crucial during this pregnancy to ensure maternal and fetal blood are compatible because there is a high risk of contact between maternal and fetal blood. An antiphospholipid antibody profile would be appropriate for evaluation of an underlying hypercoagulable disorder, such as in cases of late pregnancy loss (>10 weeks), recurrent early pregnancy loss (<10 weeks), certain preterm deliveries, or thrombosis. Although HIV testing is an important component of prenatal laboratory studies, it would not be the most important test to order at this time. A toxoplasmosis antibody test may be considered if there are signs of infection or exposure to toxoplasmosis. While cocaine use can cause placental abruption, a urine toxicology screen for cocaine is not the most pressing laboratory study to order at this time.

questions.stem: A generally healthy 50-year-old nurse presents to your clinic 1 day after sustaining an accidental needlestick containing the blood of a person with active hepatitis B virus infection who has not yet begun treatment. Your patient completed the hepatitis B (HepB) vaccine series at age 30 before beginning his first nursing job. Hepatitis serologies drawn yesterday at his place of employment were positive for hepatitis B surface antibody (>10 mIU/mL). Which one of the following would be recommended for this patient?

options.D: Both HBIG and recombinant HepB vaccine

questions.critique: Postexposure prophylaxis with hepatitis B immune globulin or hepatitis B vaccine is not recommended in those who have completed the hepatitis B vaccine series and have proven immunity to hepatitis B virus. The presence of hepatitis B surface antibody titers of >10 mIU/mL are indicative of immunity either from previous vaccination or from previous infection with hepatitis B (both hepatitis B core and surface antibodies are reactive). Hepatitis B immune globulin would be recommended in a patient with negative hepatitis B serology testing.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old female presents for a routine health maintenance examination and has no medical concerns today. She does not have a history of sexual activity, and screenings for alcohol and substance use, intimate partner violence, anxiety, depression, and suicidality are negative. An examination reveals fair skin, an elevated BMI of 31 kg/m2, and normal blood pressure. According to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force, you should recommend screening for which one of the following?
questions.critique: This patient is a healthy young adult female who presents with an elevated BMI but no other identified risk factors. The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends HIV screening for all persons 15–65 years of age (A recommendation). Cervical cancer screening is not recommended in women before age 21 (D recommendation), thus precluding the patient from screening. Chlamydia screening is recommended for all sexually active females under 25 years of age (B recommendation), but this patient has no history of sexual activity. Diabetes mellitus screening is recommended for persons 35–70 years of age with overweight or obesity (B recommendation). While the patient's age precludes her from this screening, she should be offered intensive multicomponent behavioral intervention for weight loss. Preventive counseling for skin cancer is recommended for persons with fair skin types who are 6 months to 24 years of age (B recommendation), but screening examinations are not recommended (I recommendation). This patient should also be offered screening for hepatitis C.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old male presents to your clinic with symptoms of flashing lights and increased floaters in his left eye. He does not have any pain, and he has no past medical history. His vital signs are normal. His visual acuity is at baseline when wearing his glasses, and you note a visual field defect on the left. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.critique: Family physicians frequently evaluate ophthalmologic symptoms and should be familiar with conditions requiring emergent evaluation. Peripheral flashing lights and floaters are common symptoms of retinal detachment. This patient should be referred for urgent ophthalmologic evaluation including a dilated fundoscopic examination. While positive and negative visual features are seen with migraine with aura, these features should be transient and followed by a headache typical of migraine. New-onset migraine in a 65-year-old is also uncommon and would require further evaluation. Central retinal artery occlusion, often ischemic or thromboembolic, typically occurs in those with risk factors such as diabetes mellitus, vascular disease, and tobacco use. Rapid, painless vision loss is often accompanied by afferent pupillary defect and the lesion can be visualized on a dilated fundoscopic examination as a cherry-red spot. Globe rupture is unlikely to occur without a history of trauma. Acute ischemic stroke may cause vision loss but is unlikely to cause ocular symptoms of flashing lights and floaters.

questions.stem: A 46-year-old male presents for an acute visit due to a 2-week history of low back pain. He was moving furniture when the pain started, and he felt a pop in his right lower back area. The pain is severe enough that it keeps him from standing up straight and is causing him trouble with sleeping. He does not have fever or chills, numbness, paresthesia, or weakness in his lower extremities. He has no radicular pain on a straight leg–raising test. He went to a chiropractor who did x-rays and told him he needed to get an MRI. Which one of the following, if present, would be an indication for advanced imaging?

options.C: Disrupted sleep due to nocturnal pain

questions.critique: Early advanced imaging for low back pain increases the probability of surgery and is associated with a higher use of opioids, a higher cost of care, higher pain scores, and more work absenteeism. While there is no consensus on what constitutes red flags for low back pain, new urinary retention is concerning for cauda equina syndrome. Other symptoms associated with cauda equina syndrome are bladder fullness and saddle anesthesia. The severity of pain does not correlate clearly with pathologic back pain. While cigarette use increases the chance of an underlying cancer, without a known history of cancer or other signs, it is not an indication for advanced imaging. While intravenous drug use increases the risk of epidural abscess, with no other signs of infection or history of infection, it is not solitarily an indication for advanced imaging. Nocturnal pain is not uncommon in benign low back pain and may be associated with a more specific underlying cause if the pain occurs in the second part of the night (early morning).

questions.stem: How long after intranasal administration does naloxone (Narcan) reach peak plasma concentrations?
questions.critique: After intranasal administration of naloxone, peak plasma concentrations are attained within 15–30 minutes, although its effect on the patient may be seen sooner. Absorption may be affected by substances or conditions that impact the nasal mucosa function or its blood flow. The half-life of naloxone is 2 hours, which is shorter than many long-acting opioids. Ongoing monitoring is important even after reversal of opioid respiratory depression is achieved.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old male presents for evaluation of a bulge in his groin. He reports that it is larger at the end of the day and with straining but says it often goes away when he lies down. He reports a mild dull ache that limits his ability to lift lumber at work. On examination there is no obvious bulge in the inguinal area. When the patient bears down, a bulge becomes apparent. Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step?
questions.critique: This patient presents with an inguinal hernia that is apparent on examination. When the hernia is apparent, no further imaging is needed. Recent studies have found that nearly 50% of patients sent for inguinal imaging have been unnecessarily referred. When the hernia is symptomatic or limits function, surgical referral is appropriate. If the hernia is asymptomatic, then watchful waiting would be a viable option. Physical therapy would not be appropriate for this patient with a symptomatic inguinal hernia.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old male presents to your office with his daughter who is concerned about her father's recent decline in memory. His history is notable for hypertension, urinary urgency with occasional incontinence, and GERD. On examination, you notice a slow, wide-based gait. A Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) score is 22/30. MRI of the brain is notable for diffuse dilation of the lateral, third, and fourth ventricles, and the diagnosis is confirmed with a high-volume lumbar puncture. Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this condition?
questions.critique: This patient presents with the classic triad symptoms associated with normal-pressure hydrocephalus: gait change, urinary incontinence, and dementia. MRI will show evidence of hydrocephalus, and gait changes will often improve with high-volume removal of cerebrospinal fluid. Treatment involves placing a ventriculoperitoneal shunt to relieve hydrocephalus. Carbidopa/levodopa, donepezil, levothyroxine, and surgery with chemotherapy are all treatments for other causes of dementia including Parkinson disease with Lewy body dementia, Alzheimer dementia, hypothyroidism, and brain tumor or neoplasm, respectively.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old male presents to the emergency department with an acute onset of bloody urine confirmed to be hematuria on urinalysis. He does not have any pain, fever, or recent trauma. His medical history is significant for hypertension that is managed with lisinopril (Zestril). A physical examination is unremarkable. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in evaluation?

options.B: Initiating empiric antibiotic therapy and re-evaluating in 1 week
options.D: Referring the patient for immediate cystoscopy and imaging studies

questions.critique: The American College of Physicians recommends that all adults with gross hematuria be referred for further urologic evaluation, including cystoscopy and imaging, due to the high association with malignancy. The sensitivity and specificity of cystoscopy for detecting bladder cancer are high, with a sensitivity ranging from 87% to 100% and a specificity of 64% to 100%. The positive predictive value of gross hematuria for urologic malignancy in older men is high. Gross hematuria warrants immediate investigation to rule out serious underlying conditions; thus a repeat urinalysis in 1 month would not be appropriate. Initiating empiric antibiotic therapy and re-evaluating in 1 week would not be appropriate without evidence of infection. Urine cytology has limited sensitivity and specificity and should not replace cystoscopy and imaging for the initial evaluation of hematuria.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old male presents to establish care. He smokes 1½ packs of cigarettes per day and is uncertain about cutting back or quitting. While planning to enact a patient-centered strategy to help him overcome his reluctance, you recall that evidence supports the application of a counseling technique that uses open-ended questions, affirmations, reflective listening, and summary statements to help foster a sense of patient autonomy and effect positive behavioral change. Which one of the following counseling strategies best fits this description?

options.B: The five A’s (ask, advise, assess, assist, arrange)

questions.critique: Motivational interviewing (MI) is the counseling strategy that best fits the described approach. MI is an evidence-proven strategy that is particularly helpful in situations of ambivalence toward change. In primary care settings, MI has been shown to help people achieve weight loss, improve blood pressure, and reduce alcohol use (SOR A). The key elements of MI include establishing rapport, eliciting the patient's values, asking open- ended questions, offering affirmations, practicing reflective listening, and recapping discussions with summarizing statements. The acronym OARS (open-ended questions, affirmations, reflective listening, summaries) serves as a reminder of these 4 MI components. The BATHE (background, affect, troubles, handling, empathy) model provides a structure for addressing various psychosocial problems. BATHE uses more targeted lines of questioning, in contrast to the MI approach of asking open-ended questions that give way to affirming, reflective, and summarizing statements. The five A's (ask, advise, assess, assist, arrange) approach provides a stepwise guide for assessing and counseling on health-related behaviors. In this strategy, the lines of questioning are targeted, rather than open-ended, leading to more directive recommendations and focused follow-up questions. The transtheoretical (stages of change) model can be helpful when patients are ambivalent, but this approach focuses more on identifying the patient's current level of readiness to modify a particular health behavior. The stages are precontemplation, contemplation, preparation, action, and maintenance. Identifying a patient's current stage can facilitate movement to the next stage of change and can provide a helpful framework to select another counseling strategy, such as MI.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old female with type 1 diabetes presents with a skin lesion on her leg. The lesion began as a small, dusky red, elevated nodule with a sharply circumscribed border, and slowly enlarged to become the lesion shown below. The most likely diagnosis is
questions.critique: This patient's most likely diagnosis is necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum. Its pathophysiology is poorly understood, but lesions are commonly found on the anterior and lateral surfaces of the lower legs. Lesions may begin as a small, dusky red, elevated nodule with a sharply circumscribed border. The nodule slowly enlarges, develops an irregular outline, and flattens, and has a brownish-yellow center. A reddened border may be present. Diabetic dermopathy characteristically occurs as crops of brownish atrophic lesions. In diabetic lipoatrophy the skin is depressed but otherwise normal. Diabetic rubeosis is described as reddening of the face and extremities in patients with long- standing diabetes mellitus. Eruptive xanthomas from diabetes are typically small, reddish- yellow nodules up to 0.5 cm in diameter that appear in clusters on the extensor surfaces and buttocks.

questions.stem: Metformin can cause which one of the following?
questions.critique: Asymptomatic vitamin B12 deficiency has been reported in a significant proportion of patients taking metformin (SOR A). A randomized, placebo-controlled trial showed that patients taking metformin had a 19% decrease in vitamin B12 levels and a 7% increase in absolute risk of vitamin B12 deficiency compared to the placebo group. Chromatopsia, which is a visual disturbance of color perception, is associated with use of phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors, such as sildenafil. Metformin use is not associated with ileus. Although metformin use is contraindicated in patients with impaired renal function, metformin itself does not cause renal failure. Because diarrhea occurs relatively frequently with metformin therapy, metformin use is not commonly associated with weight gain.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old female is brought to your office by her parents with fever, cough, and decreased appetite. On examination you hear crackles in the left upper lobe and a chest x-ray confirms a left upper lobe consolidation. She appears well hydrated and her vital signs are normal. On examination she is drinking water and interacting normally. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate antibiotic for this patient's condition?
questions.critique: The majority of cases of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in children are caused by viruses. If bacterial pneumonia were suspected, then antibiotics would be warranted. Given this patient's focal lung examination and x-ray findings, the most likely causative bacteria would be a typical pathogen such as Streptococcus pneumoniae or Haemophilus influenzae. The first-line choice of antibiotics would be amoxicillin for 7 days. Azithromycin is appropriate for treatment of atypical pneumonia. While cefdinir does treat the typical pathogens seen in CAP, it would not be recommended for first-line treatment due to its broader spectrum. Doxycycline is used to treat CAP but should not be used in a 3-year-old. Sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim is not indicated for CAP treatment.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old male presents to the urgent care clinic with pain in his right index finger after jamming it during basketball practice. An examination demonstrates a gross deformity of the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint of the affected finger. There is dorsal angulation of the proximal phalanx. Capillary refill, sensation, and pulses are all intact. Because the patient has pain with attempted movement of the finger, a digital block using 1% lidocaine without epinephrine is used to complete the examination. Complete x-rays, including true lateral films, reveal a very small fracture of the middle phalanx involving a minimal (<20%) area of the articular surface. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in the treatment of this patient's finger dislocation?

options.A: Discharging home, and repeating x-rays in 1 week
options.B: Reduction, followed by splinting in a flexed position and buddy taping
options.D: Referral for physical therapy with instructions to start early range-of-motion exercises
options.E: Urgent referral to an orthopedist for hand surgery

questions.critique: This patient has a dorsal dislocation of the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint of the finger, which is the most commonly dislocated joint in the body. Examination of the patient's finger reveals intact neurovascular findings. Conservative management is indicated given the limited articular surface involvement. A simple, stable dorsal PIP dislocation can be managed with in-office reduction, followed by splinting in approximately 30° of flexion for 2–4 weeks. Buddy taping is also recommended to prevent hyperextension deformities. X- rays should be repeated directly after reduction, and again in 1 week. Neither MRI nor physical therapy are indicated. Immediate surgical referral is necessary for finger fracture-dislocations that are irreducible, unstable after reduction, or involve significant soft tissue, tendon, or neurovascular injury. Additional indications include fractures involving a large portion of the articular surface (typically >30%), significant displacement or angulation (>15°), malrotation, comminution, and persistent subluxation or dislocation on imaging. Open fractures (exposed bone or joint) and injuries with vascular compromise or loss of function also require urgent surgical evaluation.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old female comes to your office because she is having moderately severe, diffuse abdominal pain with nausea and approximately 5 loose, greasy stools daily. She recently returned from a 2-week backpacking trip in Nicaragua. Stool studies are positive for Giardia. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate treatment?
questions.critique: Giardia is a parasite that causes diarrheal illness. While found globally, it is especially common in areas where sanitary conditions are poor and water treatment facilities are limited. Transmission may be waterborne, foodborne, or fecal-oral. The incubation period is usually 1–2 weeks, and symptoms include greasy diarrhea, abdominal pain, and nausea. Fever and vomiting are uncommon. While some infections are self-limited, treatment is recommended for symptomatic individuals to improve their symptoms and prevent transmission to others. Metronidazole, nitazoxanide, or tinidazole are recommended for treatment of Giardia infection. Azithromycin, ciprofloxacin, and ivermectin are antimicrobials that are commonly used to treat other causes of traveler's diarrhea.

questions.stem: A 49-year-old female with a past medical history of epilepsy presents to your office because of sadness, sleep disruption, generalized anhedonia, and a recent increase in appetite. Her Patient Health Questionnaire–9 (PHQ-9) score is elevated at 14. Which one of the following medications should be AVOIDED in this patient?
questions.critique: Antidepressants including bupropion, trazodone, and tricyclic antidepressants are associated with an increased risk of seizure and should be avoided in patients with epilepsy. In general, SSRIs or SNRIs are safer choices. Mirtazapine and vortioxetine would also be preferred choices.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old female presents for a health maintenance examination. She has no significant past medical history and no concerns. Based on U.S. Preventive Services Task Force guidelines, screening for which one of the following would be most appropriate at this time?
questions.critique: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force gives a B recommendation for screening for hepatitis C in individuals 18–79 years of age. Cervical cancer screening is not recommended until at least age 21 (A recommendation). Evidence for screening for hyperlipidemia and skin cancer is uncertain. Current evidence is insufficient to assess the balance of the benefits and harms of visual skin examination to screen for skin cancer (I recommendation), but counseling for ultraviolet exposure is recommended for persons 6 months to 24 years of age with fair skin types (B recommendation).

questions.stem: A 75-year-old male with acute confusion presents to the emergency department and is found to have a blood pressure of 280/140 mm Hg. Which one of the following potential etiologies would provide the strongest indication for lowering his systolic blood pressure to a target of 120 mm Hg within 20 minutes?
questions.critique: Treatment of acute aortic dissection includes aggressive lowering of the systolic blood pressure to 100–120 mm Hg within 20 minutes and concomitant lowering of the pulse rate to <60 beats/min to reduce shear stress on the aortic wall, minimize the risk of propagating the dissection, and reduce the risk of other complications, including rupture and aneurysmal degeneration. The optimal treatment of acute ischemic stroke, acute renal failure, hypertensive encephalopathy, and intracerebral hemorrhage requires blood pressure to be lowered more cautiously to avoid the risk of complications that may be seen with overly rapid and aggressive blood pressure–lowering.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old male sees you because he has been falling asleep at work in the afternoon. Upon questioning he notes that he is very tired all the time. Although he is in bed 8 hours per night and wakes with an alarm, he gets up 3 times nightly to urinate. He says that he has been told he snores. He does not have any constipation, dry skin, mood changes, stomach pain, changes in urine stream, shortness of breath, or chest pain. The patient is 173 cm (68 in) tall and weighs 95 kg (209 lb). His vital signs include a blood pressure of 140/88 mm Hg, a pulse rate of 79 beats/min, and a respiratory rate of 16/min. Which one of the following is most likely to yield the correct diagnosis?
questions.critique: The combination of obesity, loud snoring, and fatigue is suspicious for obstructive sleep apnea, and polysomnography is the definitive diagnostic study. A TSH level would be useful if hypothyroidism were suspected as the cause of this patient's fatigue, but it is less likely because he does not have any mood changes, dry skin, or constipation. Low vitamin B12, folate, and iron levels are also less likely based on this patient's presentation. A comprehensive metabolic panel would be useful if kidney or liver function were suspected as the cause of the patient's fatigue, but these are less likely because he does not have shortness of breath, stomach pain, or chest pain.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old female presents for a routine well child examination. She has never been sexually active and does not use illicit substances. Based on guidelines from the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force, screening patients such as this for which one of the following has the strongest evidence of benefit?
questions.critique: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) recommends screening for anxiety in children and adolescents 8–18 years of age (B recommendation). The USPSTF does recommend screening for chlamydia and gonorrhea for sexually active females in this patient's age group (B recommendation); however, this patient is not sexually active. Screening adolescents for eating disorders, elevated blood pressure, and scoliosis all have insufficient evidence to assess the benefits and harms (I recommendation).

questions.stem: A 50-year-old female presents for evaluation of unilateral shoulder pain without a history of acute injury. She has tried ibuprofen with minimal symptomatic relief. Plain radiographs of the affected shoulder taken at an urgent care clinic last week demonstrated no abnormal findings. Which one of the following physical examination findings would best support a diagnosis of adhesive capsulitis as the primary etiology of this patient's shoulder pain?

options.A: Apprehension in a 90° position with abduction and external rotation
options.B: Crepitus on passive abduction with preserved passive range of abduction
options.C: Restricted active range of abduction with preserved passive range of abduction
options.D: Restricted passive range of abduction and external rotation
options.E: Weakness on resisted abduction and external rotation

questions.critique: The hallmark finding of adhesive capsulitis, also known as frozen shoulder, is pain associated with restricted passive range of motion, particularly in external rotation, flexion, and abduction. Osteoarthritis of the glenohumeral joint also commonly causes pain and stiffness with limited passive range of motion; however, radiographs would show evidence of osteoarthritis, with joint space narrowing, sclerosis, osteophyte formation, and subchondral cystic change. With adhesive capsulitis, radiographs are typically normal, although characteristic findings are seen on MRI. Limited passive range of motion occurs with adhesive capsulitis and osteoarthritis because both conditions involve structural changes within the intra-articular space of the glenohumeral joint, thus resulting in mechanical restriction of the range of motion. Apprehension in a position of abduction and external rotation would suggest a diagnosis of shoulder instability. Crepitus on passive range of motion would suggest a mechanical abnormality within the shoulder girdle (either intra- or extra-articular) and is nonspecific to any one condition. Limited active range of motion in abduction with preserved passive range of motion is common to extra-articular sources of shoulder pain, such as rotator cuff tendinitis and impingement, subacromial bursitis, biceps tendinitis, and partial or complete rotator cuff tears. Weakness on manual muscle testing in abduction and external rotation would raise concern for a high-grade or full-thickness rotator cuff tear.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old male with a history of type 2 diabetes presents to your office because he is concerned about an ulcer on the bottom of his right foot. He is uncertain how long the ulcer has been present. He is afebrile and otherwise asymptomatic. His most recent hemoglobin A1c is 9.7% and was obtained 9 months ago. His medications include metformin, 2000 mg daily, and insulin glargine (Lantus), 34 units nightly. He does not regularly check his blood glucose levels. On examination, the patient has an ulcer on the plantar surface of the right foot overlying the head of the fifth metatarsal, measuring 1.0 cm in diameter. The ulcer appears deep with erythema that extends approximately 2.5 cm from the margin of the ulcer. A probe-to-bone test is positive. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next test to evaluate for osteomyelitis?
questions.critique: In a patient with diabetes mellitus and suspected osteomyelitis of the foot, initial evaluation should include a probe-to-bone test, a serum inflammatory marker such as an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and plain radiography. Plain radiography is the recommended initial imaging modality in the diagnosis of osteomyelitis due to its low cost, safety, and availability. If a plain radiograph suggests osteomyelitis, advanced imaging may not be necessary. While an elevated WBC count can be used to grade the severity of a diabetes-related foot infection, it can be normal in about half of patients with a severe diabetes-related foot infection and is not helpful in the diagnosis of osteomyelitis. A highly elevated ESR, however, is suggestive of an osteomyelitis infection. Superficial wound cultures are poorly sensitive and specific for identifying diabetes-related wound organisms compared to deep tissue cultures and are not used in the assessment of diabetes-related foot infections or osteomyelitis. While evaluation of peripheral vasculature and oxygenation may be warranted in the management of diabetes-related foot infections and osteomyelitis, it does not contribute to the diagnosis of osteomyelitis.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old female with hypertension sees you for routine follow-up. She has been bothered by swelling in her legs for the last several months. Her blood pressure is well controlled and she does not have any shortness of breath, cough, difficulty breathing while lying down, or pain in her legs when walking. She thinks the swelling is better earlier in the day. Her current medications include hydrochlorothiazide, lisinopril (Zestril), estrogen, levothyroxine (Synthroid), and as-needed acetaminophen. A physical examination demonstrates mild pitting edema of both legs from the ankle to the distal third of each calf. There are 2+ pedal pulses and no skin changes. Examinations of the heart and lungs are normal. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Holding estrogen, and monitoring for improvement in peripheral edema
options.B: Holding lisinopril, and monitoring for improvement in peripheral edema
options.E: Bilateral venous duplex ultrasonography with reflux

questions.critique: Adverse medication effects should always be considered when evaluating new symptoms, such as the association between estrogen use and peripheral edema. This patient's age suggests that she is likely remote from menopause, which provides a reason to consider discontinuing estrogen therapy. Peripheral edema is not associated with ACE inhibitor use. In fact, combining an ACE inhibitor with a calcium channel blocker (CCB) may decrease the edema attributable to CCB use. Ankle-brachial indices are indicated for the evaluation of suspected arterial disease. Arterial disease typically presents as lower extremity pain during activity that improves with rest. Arterial disease may lead to decreased arterial pulses. Arterial disease should be ruled out in those at risk before starting compression therapy for venous disease. Venous compression ultrasonography is indicated when deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is suspected. DVT is unlikely in the presentation of chronic bilateral lower extremity edema. DVT should be considered in acute unilateral edema, and the Wells score should be used to determine if there is an intermediate or high risk. The combination of a low-risk Wells score and a negative D-dimer level safely rules out DVT. Venous duplex ultrasonography with reflux is helpful in evaluating for chronic venous insufficiency. While this patient may have chronic venous insufficiency, her current medication use could be causing her edema. This should be evaluated before pursuing additional diagnostic testing given her lack of alarm symptoms, such as signs of heart failure or lower extremity ulcerations.

questions.stem: Which one of the following antibiotics would be recommended for the treatment of syphilis in a pregnant patient who has a history of penicillin allergy?

options.D: Penicillin G benzathine (Bicillin L-A) after desensitization

questions.critique: Parenteral penicillin G benzathine is the only therapy with documented effectiveness for treating syphilis in a pregnant person. In pregnant patients with any stage of syphilis and a reported penicillin allergy, the CDC recommends desensitization followed by treatment with penicillin G benzathine. Azithromycin and erythromycin are insufficient to cure maternal infection or treat fetal infection. There is insufficient data to recommend cephalosporins such as ceftriaxone for the treatment of maternal infections and the prevention of congenital infections. Doxycycline and tetracycline are not recommended for use in the second and third trimesters.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old male with no significant past medical history presents with his parents for a well child visit. His BMI has consistently been above the 80th percentile. His parents have both struggled with maintaining healthy weights. To help them promote a healthy lifestyle for their child, you should tell the parents which one of the following?

options.A: An hour per day of moderate or vigorous physical activity is recommended starting at age 6
options.B: Calorie restriction alone is sufficient in maintaining a healthy weight
options.C: Children should avoid video screen–based physical activity
options.D: Organic fruit juices can be provided to achieve the recommended daily servings of fruits and vegetables

questions.critique: Anticipatory guidance for lifestyle management in children should incorporate multiple approaches that address nutrition, physical activity, and behaviors that contribute to possible energy imbalance. These recommendations include 60 minutes per day of moderate to vigorous physical activity for children 6–17 years of age. Calorie restriction is beneficial in reducing weight in adolescents when combined with additional strategies, such as physical activity and behavioral changes. While excessive video screen time has been associated with increased pediatric obesity, screen-based physical activity (eg, video games that require whole-body movement) can help reduce weight in children with overweight and obesity. Children should consume 5 servings of fruits and vegetables per day. The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends against the use of fruit juices as a substitute for whole fruit due to its high calorie content.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old female presents with numbness of her bilateral feet. She first became aware of this during a recent pedicure when she had difficulty feeling the nail technician work on her toes and the plantar surface of her feet. Since that time, she has noted a feeling of "walking on cotton gauze" and has started wearing slippers around her home. She had intense pruritus of her feet approximately 6 months ago that persisted for several weeks, but it was not accompanied by paresthesia. She is otherwise in good health and participates in an aerobics class 3 days per week. She takes lisinopril (Zestril), 10 mg daily, for essential hypertension. She does not have any other symptoms. On examination, her cranial nerves, muscle strength, and reflexes are normal. Sensation is diminished over the plantar surface and toes of both feet. She has 2+ dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses bilaterally. Routine laboratory studies, including a CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, hemoglobin A1c, serum protein electrophoresis, and TSH, vitamin B12, and folate levels, are all in normal range. She is concerned that she may be developing multiple sclerosis. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step?
questions.critique: This patient demonstrates a symmetric pattern of paresthesia without red flag symptoms such as ataxia, double vision, or speech issues, which makes a lesion of the central nervous system or a nerve root less likely. Because the results of her routine laboratory studies are normal, electrodiagnostic testing would be the recommended next step. While gabapentin can be helpful in treating painful peripheral neuropathy, further workup is needed to determine the cause of this patient's symptoms. Lyme antibody testing and vitamin level testing plus supplementation may be helpful if electrodiagnostic testing exhibits an axonal pattern. Imaging of the brain or spine, such as with MRI, is not recommended in patients without central neurologic signs or asymmetry.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old female presents for evaluation of right heel pain. She recently returned from a hiking trip during which she developed progressive heel discomfort. The patient's only medication is losartan (Cozaar), which she takes for hypertension. After a thorough examination, you diagnose Achilles tendinopathy. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in diagnosis and management?

options.C: Corticosteroid injection of the Achilles tendon

questions.critique: Achilles tendinopathy is a common injury in active individuals. The diagnosis is made clinically; therefore, additional imaging is not indicated unless there is concern for an alternate diagnosis. Current recommendations for management include physical therapy for activity modification and targeted exercises. Neither complete rest nor corticosteroid injection are currently recommended for management of Achilles tendinopathy. While platelet-rich plasma injection continues to be studied in tendon injuries, it is not currently recommended as first-line management. Furthermore, studies remain mixed as to whether platelet-rich plasma injections are superior to placebo for pain control and return to normal activity.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old male with a new diagnosis of type 2 diabetes is anxious about managing his condition and uncertain about his ability to meet the demands of treatment and self-care. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate strategy to help him understand his diagnosis and follow through on lifestyle and/or medication changes?

options.A: Emphasizing the seriousness of the condition by showing pictures of patients with complications
options.B: Explaining the diagnosis and treatment plan with medical language to ensure professional communication
options.C: Supplying only detailed written instructions about his condition immediately after the appointment
options.D: Using motivational interviewing to identify what is most important to the patient and create impetus for change

questions.critique: Motivational interviewing is the communication strategy with the best evidence of leading to a change in behavior. Scare tactics have not been shown to be effective. Physicians should speak in language that the patient can understand rather than use jargon. Supplying too much detail may overwhelm the patient, especially if it is not tailored to the individual's goals.

questions.stem: A generally healthy 15-year-old female presents to your office with a history of frequent, generalized abdominal pain and loose stools daily for the last few months. She has not noted any relationship to food or a specific frequency. She has not had any previous surgeries and is up to date on routine vaccinations. At her 14-year-old well child visit, her height was at the 50th percentile and her weight was at the 60th percentile. Today, her height is at the 35th percentile and her weight at the 20th percentile. She has had a weight loss of 1 kg (2 lb). Her vital signs are normal. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Reassurance and dietary monitoring to evaluate for a relationship to symptoms

questions.critique: Patients with classic symptoms of diarrhea, abdominal pain, weight loss, bloating, malabsorption, and failure to thrive should undergo evaluation for celiac disease. Serologic testing for IgA tissue transglutaminase (tTG) is the preferred initial screening in all age groups due to its high sensitivity (95%) and specificity (95%). When celiac disease is strongly suspected, total IgA levels should also be obtained because 2%–3% of these patients will have IgA deficiency. If IgA tTG serology is positive, most patients will require esophagogastroduodenoscopy with small-bowel biopsy for confirmation, but it is not used as an initial test and some organizations state that a no-biopsy technique is safe for children with very high IgA tTG levels. This patient has classic signs of malabsorption and unexplained weight loss, indicating need for further evaluation. Reassurance would be inappropriate at this time. Fecal fat testing may be useful in the workup of suspected malabsorption symptoms, but the initial test is usually a qualitative assessment of fat in a single specimen because it is less expensive and easier to perform than 72-hour testing. If high suspicion remains after negative initial testing, a 72-hour fecal fat test should be performed. Carbohydrate breath testing can be used to evaluate for suspected small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO). SIBO generally occurs in individuals with abnormal anatomy or other risk factors for abnormal intestinal mobility, including diabetes mellitus or scleroderma. The majority of patients with SIBO present with bloating and flatulence.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old male asks you about testosterone supplementation during a routine office visit. He reports that he has had decreased energy, decreased libido, and loss of upper and lower body strength over the last year. He would like to have his testosterone level checked today. Which one of the following symptoms, if present in this patient, would be most concerning for testosterone deficiency?

options.C: Loss of axillary and pubic hair
options.D: Thinning of hair at the temples

questions.critique: Loss of axillary and pubic hair is one of the more specific symptoms of male hypogonadism. Memory issues have myriad etiologies; they may be secondary to attention disorders, mood disorders, sleep apnea, or other neurologic disorders. Fatigue or diminished stamina may be a symptom of male hypogonadism but also can be indicative of other endocrine, musculoskeletal, or rheumatologic disorders. Thinning hair at the temples may occur with aging and with androgenic alopecia. Thinning of the eyebrows may be related to thyroid disorders.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old female is brought to your office by her parents for a preschool examination. She had a normal birth and is up to date on her vaccinations. On examination she appears to be healthy, and her development is appropriate for her age. She is in the 50th percentile for height and weight. You note a grade 1–2/6 continuous murmur best heard in the infraclavicular region when she is upright. The murmur changes slightly in intensity with head movement and is absent when she is supine. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Reassuring the parents that this is a benign venous hum that needs no follow-up
options.B: Scheduling a follow-up appointment in 1 month to recheck the murmur
options.C: Recommending that the patient undergo echocardiography when she reaches adulthood
options.D: Sending the patient for evaluation for repair of a patent ductus arteriosus

questions.critique: A venous hum is an innocent heart murmur that is caused by vein vibration. It is commonly heard in children and may appear between 3 and 6 years of age. It is typically audible only when the child is upright, and changes with head movement. In a child with a venous hum, reassurance of the parents is appropriate, and no follow-up is necessary.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old female presents to your office after an episode of anaphylaxis. She has a known tree nut allergy and developed oral swelling, dyspnea, and chest pain after eating a muffin from a coffee shop. Her symptoms initially improved after she used her epinephrine auto-injector but began to worsen 10 minutes later. She used a second dose of her auto-injector 10 minutes prior to presentation and is feeling much better. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Starting famotidine (Pepcid), 20 mg daily, and follow-up in 1 week
options.B: Starting prednisone, 40 mg for 5 days, and follow-up in 5 days
options.C: Starting fexofenadine (Allegra), 180 mg daily, and immediate follow-up with an allergist
options.D: Transfer to the emergency department (ED) with observation for 1–2 hours prior to discharge
options.E: Transfer to the ED with observation for 6–12 hours prior to discharge

questions.critique: This patient experienced anaphylaxis due to her tree nut allergy. Her anaphylaxis was refractory and required 2 doses of epinephrine for treatment. Although she is feeling better, patients with refractory anaphylaxis should be transferred to a higher level of care, such as an emergency department, for further evaluation and management. Patients who have had severe or refractory anaphylaxis are at risk for a biphasic reaction and should be observed for 6–12 hours before discharge. Although medications such as H1- and H2-blockers and corticosteroids can be helpful in the postanaphylaxis care of patients, they are not the mainstay of therapy for patients who have had severe anaphylaxis.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old male has been admitted to the hospital for hypotension and electrolyte disturbances due to chronic diarrhea. He was stabilized in the ICU and transferred to the inpatient floor. His gastroenterologist is asking for a colonoscopy with biopsies. His medical history is significant for congenital heart disease with a mechanical aortic valve replacement, an embolic stroke resulting in right hemiparesis, a deep vein thrombosis, and a solitary kidney. He is currently anticoagulated with warfarin and has an INR of 2.6. He has a creatinine level of 2.8 mg/dL (N 0.7–1.3) and a glomerular filtration rate of 28 mL/min/1.73 m2. In addition to holding warfarin for the planned colonoscopy, how should you manage this patient’s anticoagulation while he is hospitalized?

options.A: Resuming warfarin post procedure with a goal INR of 2.0–3.0
options.B: Vitamin K for reversal, and resuming warfarin post procedure with a goal INR of 2.5–3.5
options.E: A heparin drip titrated to achieve therapeutic aPTT or anti-factor Xa levels

questions.critique: This patient requires a procedure with a high likelihood of bleeding and should not be anticoagulated at the time of the procedure. He also has a high likelihood of thromboembolic disease and should receive bridging with a heparin drip until his INR has normalized for the planned procedure. With his mechanical heart valve, the correct INR goal is 2.5–3.5. Reversal with vitamin K is not necessary because he is stable and not actively bleeding. He is not a candidate for enoxaparin due to his glomerular filtration rate. Heparin, 5000 units subcutaneously, is a prophylactic dose, not a treatment dose.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old male is brought to the emergency department by his parents after tripping and falling on his hand in an outstretched position. An x-ray reveals a buckle (torus) fracture of the distal radial metaphysis. Which one of the following management strategies would be most appropriate?

options.A: A short arm splint for 3 weeks with follow-up radiography
