options.B: A short arm splint for 3 weeks with follow-up radiography only if pain or tenderness persists
options.C: A short arm cast for 6 weeks with follow-up radiography
options.D: A short arm cast for 6 weeks with follow-up radiography only if pain or tenderness persists

questions.critique: Buckle (torus) fractures are incomplete compression fractures that cause cortical bulging of the bone without cortical disruption. They are common in children because of the elasticity of their bones. Distal radius buckle fractures should be treated with short arm immobilization using a short arm splint, soft cast, or wrap for 3 weeks. Complete immobilization with a cast is unnecessary. According to the Choosing Wisely campaign, follow-up radiography is unnecessary unless the patient continues to experience pain or tenderness.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old female presents for a wellness visit while home on college break. According to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force, which one of the following screenings is indicated?
questions.critique: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) recommends screening for unhealthy drug use in adults ≥18 years of age (B recommendation). Unhealthy drug use is defined as using psychoactive medications for purposes other than as prescribed or illegally obtaining drugs for personal use. For this patient's age group, the USPSTF has determined the evidence to be insufficient to recommend for or against screening for cavities or periodontal disease, dyslipidemia, skin cancer, or suicide risk (I recommendation).

questions.stem: A 33-year-old oyster fisherman presents because of a 3-day history of diarrhea. He describes frequent, large-volume, watery stool that is milky white. His vital signs are normal and he appears nontoxic. Infection with which one of the following pathogens is most likely?
questions.critique: In patients presenting with large-volume rice-water or milky-white stools, the most likely pathogen is Vibrio species. Patients with this presentation should be tested for infection regardless of exposure to undercooked shellfish or brackish waters. Guidelines from the Infectious Diseases Society of America recommend testing for infection with Clostridioides difficile, enteropathogenic Escherichia coli, enterotoxigenic E coli, and Yersinia enterocolitica in patients with diarrhea and fever, bloody or mucoid stools, signs of sepsis, or severe abdominal cramping or tenderness.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old female with Down syndrome presents for a health maintenance examination. She does not have any new symptoms or concerns today. Her only medication is an etonogestrel implant (Nexplanon) that was placed 2 years ago. She is monogamous with a male sex partner. On examination you note normal vital signs with a BMI of 28 kg/m2. At her most recent laboratory evaluation 2 years ago, she had an LDL-cholesterol level of 104 mg/dL, a fasting glucose level of 89 mg/dL, and a TSH level of 2.67 μU/mL (N 0.4–4.2). Which one of the following screening evaluations should you order today?

options.A: The National Task Group–Early Detection Screen for Dementia tool

questions.critique: In 2017, the U.S. nonprofit group Global Down Syndrome Foundation convened a workgroup to create an evidence-based guideline for clinicians and adults with Down syndrome and their supporters. This guideline was published in 2020 and includes responses to 10 questions that were created using the Population/Intervention/Comparator/Outcome (PICO) format. They address screening for conditions known to be of higher prevalence in individuals with Down syndrome. In the United States, hypothyroidism is common in individuals with Down syndrome, with an estimated prevalence of approximately 50% in adults >30 years of age. Screening is therefore recommended with TSH testing every 1–2 years starting at age 21. This patient's TSH level was last checked 2 years ago and is due today. Dementia occurs on average at younger ages in those with Down syndrome than those without Down syndrome; however, it is rare before the age of 40. If any concerning symptoms arise in this age group, a thorough investigation for alternative causes is recommended. Routine screening for dementia is recommended beginning at age 40 for individuals with Down syndrome. A validated tool such as the National Task Group–Early Detection Screen for Dementia should be used. Cardiovascular disease prevention is recommended for individuals with Down syndrome per U.S. Preventive Services Task Force guidelines. This patient's LDL-cholesterol level was last checked 2 years ago and is not due for recheck today. The prevalence of osteoporosis in individuals with Down syndrome has been estimated with a wide degree of variation (1.4%–45.1%). There is no evidence that DXA screening at ages younger than recommended for adults without Down syndrome (age 65) is of benefit. Although atlantoaxial instability (AAI) is known to have a higher prevalence in those with Down syndrome than those without Down syndrome, no studies have assessed the use of cervical spine radiography for diagnosing AAI. Screening radiography is therefore n

questions.stem: You are participating in a continuing medical education (CME) course at a Colorado ski resort that is 9600 ft (2926 m) above sea level. You and your travel companions have had intermittent issues with headaches, loss of appetite, and difficulty sleeping. On the third day, one of your companions starts to experience shortness of breath that is not relieved with rest. Their symptoms worsen throughout the day and they become slightly tachycardic. Later that evening, they develop a cough that produces frothy sputum. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step?

options.E: Oxygen supplementation, and descending to a lower elevation

questions.critique: The risk for acute mountain sickness increases when sleeping above altitudes of about 8200 ft (2499 m). Symptoms can include difficulty sleeping, headaches, and decreased appetite. This patient has more severe symptoms of high-altitude pulmonary edema (HAPE), which is a medical emergency, and should begin supplemental oxygen and descend to a lower altitude if possible. Acetazolamide can be used to prevent acute mountain sickness, but it is not a treatment. Dexamethasone is a supplemental treatment for high-altitude cerebral edema. Nifedipine can be used in the prevention of HAPE and as a treatment in emergency situations in which descent or a hyperbaric chamber is not available. In this case, the patient is at a modern ski resort and descent should be feasible. COVID-19 infection is unlikely in this scenario.

questions.stem: Which one of the following antibiotics is most commonly associated with Stevens-Johnson syndrome?
questions.critique: Antibiotics are the most common cause of Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) and toxic epidermal necrolysis, accounting for more than one-quarter of cases worldwide. Sulfonamide antibiotics, such as sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim, are associated with the majority of antibiotic-associated SJS cases worldwide. Antibiotic stewardship is always important, and consideration for both relatively benign and life-threatening side effects should be considered with every prescription written.

questions.stem: During an annual visit, a 62-year-old female reports an episode of vaginal spotting that lasted for a few days in May. It is now July and she has not had another recurrence. A transvaginal ultrasound shows an endometrial stripe of 4 mm, no fibroids, and no polyps. Which one of the following would be most appropriate regarding follow-up evaluation?
questions.critique: Transvaginal ultrasonography or endometrial biopsy are both appropriate initial diagnostic tests for postmenopausal bleeding. An ultrasound showing an endometrial stripe ≤4 mm is associated with a >99% negative predictive value for endometrial cancer and no further evaluation is needed unless the bleeding is recurrent. If bleeding is persistent or recurrent, then a tissue sample would be appropriate. If an endometrial biopsy is not helpful and bleeding continues to persist or recur, then further evaluation with hysteroscopy and curettage would be indicated.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old male presents with shoulder pain after having fallen on his arm in an outstretched position. He reports a sensation of catching and popping in the shoulder. With the patient seated and hands placed on his hips, you apply force to the elbow and direct the humerus anteriorly and superiorly, while asking the patient to resist your motion, which produces pain in the affected shoulder. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.critique: This patient's history of a catching or popping sensation combined with a positive anterior slide test, as described in the physical examination, is highly suggestive of a glenoid labral tear. This combination of history and the positive anterior slide test has a positive likelihood ratio of 6.0 in diagnosing a glenoid labral tear. Acromioclavicular joint arthritis presents with superior shoulder pain and tenderness over the acromioclavicular joint, often with a positive cross-body adduction test. Adhesive capsulitis is characterized by diffuse shoulder pain and restricted passive range of motion. Glenohumeral osteoarthritis usually presents with a gradual onset of pain and loss of motion, particularly in older patients. A rotator cuff tear typically presents with pain and weakness, especially with overhead activities, and positive findings on the empty can and external rotation tests. Acromioclavicular joint arthritis, adhesive capsulitis, glenohumeral osteoarthritis, and rotator cuff tears are not associated with catching or popping sensations, and the crank and anterior slide tests are not specific for these conditions.

questions.stem: According to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force, which one of the following is the recommended next step for adults who screen positive for unhealthy alcohol use?

options.E: Referral for alcohol use disorder treatment

questions.critique: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends screening all adults for unhealthy alcohol use and implementing brief counseling interventions for those who screen positive (B recommendation). The screening, brief intervention, and referral to treatment (SBIRT) approach has been shown to decrease the number of patients with risky drinking after the intervention. Motivational interviewing is often used for brief counseling in primary care settings. Cognitive behavioral therapy, mindfulness-based therapy, and interpersonal psychotherapy are different psychotherapy approaches that are used as longer-term counseling methods and would not be appropriate for use as brief counseling interventions for adults with unhealthy alcohol use. Patients identified as having alcohol use disorder (AUD) by DSM-5 criteria should be referred for treatment.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old female presents with constipation and severe anal pain following bowel movements. On examination, you observe an anal fissure. In addition to increasing fluids, fiber, and warm sitz baths, which one of the following would be effective and least likely to result in side effects?
questions.critique: For anal fissures, topical sphincter relaxants applied externally around the anus 2–4 times per day help reduce sphincter tone and promote healing. Topical calcium channel blockers such as diltiazem are best tolerated. Topical nitroglycerin is also effective but is associated with more side effects such as headaches and lightheadedness, which results in 1 in 5 patients stopping therapy. Botulinum toxin is also effective. Topical capsaicin is an irritant. Oral calcium channel blockers are not effective for treatment of anal fissures.

questions.stem: Which one of the following interventions lowers the risk of diverticulitis?

options.A: Avoiding consumption of popcorn, nuts, and seeds

questions.critique: Diverticulitis is an inflammation of diverticula caused by a protrusion through the intestinal wall. Risk factors include constipation, a low-fiber diet, increasing age, red meat consumption, smoking, obesity, lack of exercise, aspirin and NSAID use, and genetic susceptibility. Consuming a vegetarian diet may help to prevent diverticulitis. Red meat intake, long-term NSAID use, and tobacco use are risk factors for diverticulitis. After years of research, it was concluded that consumption of nuts, seeds, and popcorn is not a risk factor for diverticulitis (SOR B). The incidence of diverticulitis is increasing, yet screening is not recommended. Tenderness located in the left lower quadrant of the abdomen is the most specific finding of diverticulitis. Evaluation includes a CBC, a basic metabolic panel, a C-reactive protein level, and urine analysis. CT with contrast is the imaging modality of choice if the diagnosis is uncertain. Treatment is based on severity and includes oral and intravenous antibiotics, bowel rest and a clear liquid diet, and surgical management for abscess formation or peritonitis as well as for prevention of recurrences.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old male presents to your office because he has been experiencing severe, stabbing pain that feels like an electric shock on the left side of his face in his cheek, lips, and chin. The episodes are usually triggered by light touch or eating and last for a few seconds. Which one of the following cranial nerves is associated with this disorder?
questions.critique: Trigeminal neuralgia is characterized by severe paroxysms of pain in the distribution of 1 or more branches of cranial nerve V. Impairment of cranial nerve VII results in paralysis of facial muscles, such as Bell palsy. Impairment of cranial nerve X may cause dysphagia and dysphonia, cranial nerve XI involvement results in neck weakness, and cranial nerve XII damage leads to tongue paralysis.

questions.stem: Which one of the following social determinants of health has a protective effective against Alzheimer disease and related types of dementia?
questions.critique: Social determinants of health (SDOH) are the conditions in which people are born, work, live, and age as well as the wider set of forces and systems that shape the conditions of daily life. Several SDOH affect cognition and risk of Alzheimer disease and related dementias (ADRDs). For example, higher levels of social engagement have a protective effect against ADRDs. Conversely, SDOH such as decreasing BMI with older age, early-life adversity, low education level, food insecurity, midlife obesity, manual labor, and low socioeconomic status are associated with a greater risk of ADRDs.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old male with a history of type 2 diabetes comes to your office for evaluation of muscle aches and fatigue. His current medications include rosuvastatin (Crestor), dapagliflozin (Farxiga), and semaglutide (Ozempic). Over the past month he has noticed increasing pain in his hips, thighs, and shoulders without any apparent trigger. He feels so stiff in the morning that it is becoming difficult to get out of bed. On examination his strength is diminished (4/5) in the proximal muscle groups. You do not note any swelling or redness of any joints, muscles, or extremities. He has normal reflexes and no clonus. Laboratory Findings Hemoglobin A1c 6.4% Hemoglobin 12.2 g/dL (N 13.2–16.6) Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (N 0.7–1.3) Potassium 4.2 mEq/L (N 3.5–5.1) Creatine kinase 84 U/L (N 55–170) Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 88 mm/hr (N 0–20) C-reactive protein 9.2 mg/L (N 0–0.3) Antinuclear antibody <1:40 (N <1:40) Which one of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's myalgias?
questions.critique: These findings are concerning for polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR), an inflammatory condition that typically affects adults ≥50 years of age. This patient's proximal muscle pain, fatigue, and decreased proximal muscle strength are consistent with this diagnosis, as are the elevated inflammatory markers (erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein level) and mild anemia. A normal creatine kinase level is expected in patients with PMR. PMR is diagnosed clinically and treated with oral glucocorticoids. Rapid improvement with therapy is expected; if improvement does not occur, an alternate diagnosis should be explored. Giant cell arteritis, also known as temporal arteritis, is associated with PMR. It causes temporal headaches, visual changes, and constitutional symptoms. Early identification and treatment are essential to prevent permanent vision change. Fibromyalgia does not cause elevated inflammatory markers. Polymyositis and statin- induced myopathy may cause myalgias but are typically associated with elevations in creatine kinase, which this patient does not have. Almost all patients with systemic lupus erythematosus have an elevated antinuclear antibody titer, which this patient also does not have.

questions.stem: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends which one of the following regarding screening asymptomatic adolescents and adults for herpes simplex virus?
questions.critique: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force concluded with moderate certainty that the harms outweighed the benefits for routine, population-based serologic screening for infection with herpes simplex virus (HSV) in asymptomatic adolescents and adults, including pregnant patients (D recommendation). High false-positive rates are common in asymptomatic patients who undergo serologic testing for infection with HSV. In addition, treatment is not recommended in asymptomatic patients with a positive test.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child is brought to the urgent care clinic by their parents with a 2-day history of abdominal pain and vomiting. The child has been irritable over the past 2 days with intermittent abdominal pain and several episodes of associated emesis, and just had a bloody stool that the parents describe as resembling jelly. The child appears uncomfortable but is alert and afebrile, with normal blood pressure. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.critique: Intussusception is a condition in which a part of the intestine slides or "telescopes" into an adjacent section. It presents with a classic triad of intermittent abdominal pain, emesis, and blood-tinged mucous stools that are sometimes called "red currant jelly" stools. It most commonly occurs at the ileocolic junction and typically affects infants and toddlers. The initial evaluation begins with ultrasonography. An enema with air or contrast can both confirm the diagnosis and reverse the intussusception. If this procedure does not relieve the condition, surgical correction is necessary. Necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe intestinal infection that typically presents with severe abdominal pain, lethargy, and unstable vital signs. Pyloric stenosis is notable for projectile vomiting, but not bloody stools. Volvulus typically involves a sudden onset of vomiting and abdominal pain and even distension, but without bloody stools.

questions.stem: A generally healthy 54-year-old male with a 20-pack-year smoking history presents to discuss smoking cessation. His first grandchild was just born and he is eager for help in quitting cigarette smoking. He quit smoking 2 years ago using nicotine gum (Nicorette Gum), but resumed smoking 5 months later. He has also explored telephone helplines and support groups, but did not find them beneficial. He currently smokes 1 pack of cigarettes per day. Which one of the following would be most likely to help this patient quit smoking?
questions.critique: Seven pharmacotherapies are approved by the FDA for smoking cessation, including bupropion, varenicline, and 5 forms of nicotine replacement therapy (NRT). All of these have been shown to be beneficial in smoking cessation compared to placebo. Compared to NRT and bupropion, varenicline appears to be the most effective monotherapy with a number needed to treat of 7. Recent meta-analyses suggest that a combination of pharmacotherapies is more effective than monotherapy. Varenicline plus NRT is more likely to result in sustained smoking cessation compared to bupropion plus NRT.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old female returns to discuss management of her recurrent nephrolithiasis. She has a history of calcium stones and just recently passed her fourth stone this year despite appropriate lifestyle changes, including a fluid intake of 3 L per day and a diet high in fiber and low in animal protein. She would like a medication to help prevent the development of more kidney stones. She has a past medical history of asthma for which she takes a budesonide/formoterol inhaler (Symbicort) as needed. Today, her vital signs and a physical examination are normal. A recent basic metabolic panel was normal. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate prevention strategy?
questions.critique: Lifestyle changes such as appropriate fluid intake (2.5–3.0 L of liquid per day), a diet high in fiber and low in animal protein, lower sodium intake (<4–5 g per day), and moderate calcium intake (1200 mg per day) can help reduce recurrence of calcium stones. However, if patients continue to develop recurrent calcium stones, it is reasonable to initiate pharmacologic therapy. Appropriate medications include high-dose thiazide diuretics, allopurinol, and potassium citrate. Furosemide would not be appropriate as it has been associated with kidney stone formation. Magnesium oxide has not been shown to decrease the risk of stone development. Tamsulosin can be used to help with stone expulsion, but it does not help to prevent stones.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old female is brought to the emergency department after her neighbor found her on the floor in her home. She is drowsy and confused, and unable to provide any history. An initial laboratory evaluation reveals the following: Glucose 95 mg/dL BUN 9 mg/dL (N 6–20) Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (N 0.5–1.1) Estimated glomerular filtration rate 63 mL/min/1.73 m2 Sodium 118 mEq/L (N 135–145) Potassium 3.0 mEq/L (N 3.3 –5.0) Chloride 88 mEq/L (N 101–115) Bicarbonate 22 mEq/L (N 16–30) Anion gap 8 mEq/L (N 5–14) Calcium 8.8 mg/dL (N 8.4–10.5) Which one of the following would be the most appropriate intervention at this time?
questions.critique: This patient presents with symptomatic severe hyponatremia (sodium level <125 mEq/L). The most appropriate intervention at this time is a 100-mL bolus of 3% hypertonic saline (SOR B), which may be repeated up to 2 times at 10-minute intervals with the goal of increasing the sodium level by 4–6 mEq/L. Serum sodium levels should be monitored after every bolus and every 6 hours during the first 24 hours of treatment (SOR C). Avoiding rapid correction of severe hyponatremia is important to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome. Depending on risk factors, sodium correction should be limited to 8–12 mEq/L over 24 hours or 18 mEq/L over 48 hours (SOR C). The use of normal saline is appropriate treatment for nonsevere hypovolemic hyponatremia (a sodium level ≥125 mEq/L but <135 mEq/L). Intravenous desmopressin (DDAVP) is indicated for treatment of diabetes insipidus, which usually presents with high- normal sodium levels or hypernatremia. Desmopressin may also be given with 5% dextrose in water if overcorrection of severe hyponatremia has occurred.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old construction worker presents to your office with an acute onset of lower back pain that began 3 days ago. The patient describes the pain as a burning sensation that radiates down his left leg. He has no bowel or bladder dysfunction and has been in good health. A straight leg–raising test is positive at 45° on the left without motor deficit. Which one of the following does evidence support as the first step in treatment of this patient's condition?
questions.critique: In the absence of red flags (fracture, malignancy, epidural abscess, or cauda equina syndrome), the approach to acute lumbar radiculopathy should be conservative. Most patients improve over the first 2 weeks, and there is level B evidence that mobilization and manipulation is of benefit. Bed rest was previously recommended, but current data supports allowing the patient to be as active as the pain allows. While corticosteroids, NSAIDs, and muscle relaxants are often used, evidence to do so is lacking. In the subacute stage, neurodynamic mobilization and strength training could be added. If there is no improvement over 6 weeks, then MRI should be ordered.

questions.stem: A 46-year-old male with chronic hypertension presents to your clinic for routine follow-up. Due to issues with insurance coverage, he has not been taking his antihypertensive medications for the last 2 months. Today, he is asymptomatic and an examination is normal, except for a blood pressure of 162/100 mm Hg, which is confirmed on repeat testing. In addition to addressing insurance and social determinants of health, which one of the following would be most appropriate for this patient?

options.A: Monotherapy with a calcium channel blocker (CCB)
options.C: Combination therapy with a CCB and an ACE inhibitor
options.D: Combination therapy with a diuretic and a β-blocker
options.E: Combination therapy with a diuretic and an aldosterone antagonist

questions.critique: The vast majority of patients treated for essential hypertension will require 2 or more agents to achieve adequate blood pressure control. Combination therapy with a calcium channel blocker and an ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker (ARB) would be most appropriate for this patient with stage 2 hypertension. Stage 2 hypertension is defined as a systolic blood pressure ≥160 mm Hg or a diastolic blood pressure ≥100 mm Hg (or a systolic blood pressure >20 mm Hg above goal or a diastolic blood pressure >10 mm Hg above goal). Race-based prescribing, which advises preferential use of thiazide or calcium channel blockers for Black patients, has demonstrated insufficient blood pressure control and is thus falling out of favor. β-Blockers are not used as first-line treatment for hypertension, but they may be indicated in patients with specific comorbidities, such as coronary artery disease or heart failure. Aldosterone antagonists are also not typically used as first-line treatment for hypertension, but they may be used if multiple other drug classes cannot achieve adequate blood pressure control. Monotherapy is unlikely to produce adequate blood pressure control in this patient.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old female presents to your office with her mother who is concerned about her daughter's recent 15-lb weight loss and severe fatigue. After ruling out other etiologies, you diagnose anorexia nervosa. Which one of the following findings, if present in this patient, would indicate the need for inpatient care?
questions.critique: A potassium level of 2.6 mEq/L would indicate the need for inpatient care of this patient's anorexia nervosa. Eating disorders can often be treated in the outpatient setting with a multidisciplinary care team including physicians, therapists, and dietitians. However, certain findings, including severe hypokalemia, require higher levels of care. While hair loss, muscle atrophy, parotid gland enlargement, and elevated sodium can all be found in patients with eating disorders, severe electrolyte abnormalities can lead to life-threatening cardiac complications and require immediate treatment.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old female presents to your office for an annual wellness visit. She reports a family history of Lynch syndrome and recently tested positive herself. Which one of the following should you recommend regarding colon cancer screening in this patient?
questions.critique: Hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer (HNPCC), formerly called Lynch syndrome, is a hereditary cancer syndrome that increases the risk for several types of cancer, especially colon cancer. Guidelines recommend that patients with HNPCC should start colon cancer screening with colonoscopy between 20 and 25 years of age and repeat every 1–2 years. Patients at average risk for colon cancer should begin colon cancer screening at age 45. Fecal occult blood testing, stool DNA testing, and sigmoidoscopy are not adequate screening tests in this high-risk population. Lynch syndrome is associated with increased risk for several other cancers including endometrial, ovarian, stomach, small-bowel, and pancreatic.

questions.stem: A 53-year-old male without a significant past medical history presents to the emergency department for evaluation of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. Pertinent findings on presentation include notable tenderness in the epigastrium and a lipase level >3 times the upper limit of normal. He is admitted for acute pancreatitis. Intravenous lactated Ringer solution, antiemetics, and pain control are initiated. At the time of your evaluation he has normal vital signs and reports improved symptoms, although you still note abdominal tenderness. When entering admission orders for this patient, which one of the following diets would be most appropriate?
questions.critique: Pancreatitis is associated with an intense inflammatory response that increases caloric needs, and nutrition management is a key component of care. Acute pancreatitis is also associated with reduced perfusion of the intestinal mucosa, leading to tissue damage and increased intestinal permeability. Enteral feeding provides calories and increases splanchnic blood flow, leading to improved integrity of the bowel wall and improved gut motility. Patients with acute pancreatitis benefit from early enteral feeding within 24 hours of admission. A Cochrane review found that enteral nutrition has been associated with lower mortality, less multiple organ failure, and less systemic infection compared with parenteral nutrition. In severe acute pancreatitis, the benefits of enteral nutrition appear to be even more notable with a relative risk of death of 0.18 (95% CI 0.06 to 0.58). Liquid and soft diets have no evidence of benefit over a low-fat solid diet, and some studies found a shorter length of stay in patients started on solid diets rather than liquid diets. If patients cannot tolerate an oral diet within 72 hours, enteral nutrition should be provided via nasogastric or nasojejunal tube. Parenteral nutrition should be administered in patients who cannot tolerate tube feeding. Low-carbohydrate diets are not recommended because they are often high in fat and may worsen pancreatic inflammation.

questions.stem: A healthy 57-year-old female presents for an annual health maintenance visit. Her last menstrual period was 2 years ago. She exercises 5 times per week, does not smoke, and rarely drinks alcohol. Her BMI is 23 kg/m2. Her 80-year-old mother has no history of fractures, but takes alendronate (Fosamax), 70 mg weekly. According to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force, which one of the following would be the preferred strategy to screen for osteoporosis in this patient?

options.A: Using the FRAX tool now to determine the need for DXA screening
options.B: Using the FRAX tool at age 65 to determine the need for DXA screening

questions.critique: According to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF), women ≥65 years of age should be screened for osteoporosis using DXA bone mineral density testing, regardless of fracture risk assessment. Postmenopausal women <65 years of age with 1 or more additional risk factors such as smoking, excessive alcohol consumption, or parental history of hip fracture should also be screened with DXA. This patient does not have any significant risk factors. Tools such as FRAX can be used to help counsel patients regarding the future risk of fracture, but they are not intended to be used to determine who should receive further screening with DXA.

questions.stem: You are evaluating a patient with acute-on-chronic kidney disease and a serum potassium level of 6.5 mEq/L (N 3.3–5.0). In addition to peaked T waves, which one of the following additional electrocardiogram changes is most likely to be present?
questions.critique: Hyperkalemia (potassium level >5.0 mEq/L) can occur with impaired renal excretion, transcellular shifts, and increased potassium intake. Management of severe hyperkalemia includes using diagnostic EKG to identify cardiac conditions requiring emergent treatment. EKG changes associated with hyperkalemia include peaked T waves, prolonged PR interval, loss of P waves, and prolonged QRS interval. Hyperkalemia resulting in prolonged QRS interval, bradycardia, and junctional rhythm is associated with adverse events, including ventricular fibrillation, cardiopulmonary resuscitation, and death within 6 hours of measurement. Findings associated with hypokalemia (potassium level <3.5 mEq/L) include flattened T waves, prolonged QT interval, ST segment depression, U waves, and T- wave inversion. Delta waves are not associated with potassium disorders but are present in Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome.

questions.stem: A 15-year-old male comes to your clinic 30 minutes after being hit in the face by a baseball during practice. He is alert and answers questions appropriately. After clearing the blood, you find that his upper front tooth (No. 8) is quite loose and the root is visible. The tooth is lying lower than his other teeth. The tooth appears intact and without fracture. There is no crepitus or pain when palpating the alveolar and mandibular bones. You immediately call his dentist to arrange an emergency appointment. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step prior to sending the patient to the dentist's office?

options.B: Repositioning the tooth in the socket and having the patient bite down on gauze
options.C: Gently removing the tooth and placing it in saline or milk

questions.critique: For dislodged or fully avulsed permanent teeth, immediate reimplantation or repositioning is recommended (SOR C). Time is imperative in saving teeth. If reimplantation does not occur within 1 hour, there is little likelihood that it will be successful. If possible, replace the tooth in its anatomic position and have the patient bite down on a washcloth or gauze to keep it in place. It is not advised to remove a loose tooth, as the safest medium for the tooth is the socket. If the tooth is fully avulsed, hold it by the crown, rinse with saline, and reimplant. A tooth that cannot be reimplanted should be placed in a saline solution or milk. Antibiotic treatment is not imperative at this time. Without evidence of a dental or bony fracture, immediate imaging is not necessary as it would delay reimplantation and referral to a dentist.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old female presents for follow-up of laboratory studies. At her last visit, she presented with palpitations, anxiety, and unintentional weight loss. An examination was notable for exophthalmos. Her laboratory results show a very low TSH level with elevated free T4 and normal total T3 levels. A radioactive iodine uptake scan demonstrates a high, homogeneous uptake of iodine. Which one of the following medications is the most appropriate treatment for this condition?
questions.critique: This patient has hyperthyroidism, specifically Graves disease. Diffusely high radioactive iodine uptake differentiates Graves disease from other thyroid conditions, including toxic adenoma (high uptake concentrated in one spot), toxic multinodular goiter (high uptake in multiple spots), and thyroiditis (low uptake). Treatment options for Graves disease include antithyroid medications, radioactive iodine ablation, and surgical thyroidectomy. The most appropriate treatment is usually determined based on the risks and benefits of each option and patient preference. Methimazole is the preferred antithyroid medication for treatment of Graves disease due to its improved safety profile compared to propylthiouracil, which has a greater risk of severe liver injury. Pregnant patients in the first trimester should be treated with propylthiouracil because it has a lower teratogenic risk than methimazole. Levothyroxine is used to treat hypothyroidism. Prednisone is used to treat severe hyperthyroidism, such as thyroid storm. A calcium channel blocker such as verapamil would be indicated if a β-blocker is contraindicated or not tolerated.

questions.stem: An 85-year-old male on the third day of hospitalization for the treatment of pneumonia develops symptoms of altered mental status. Evaluation of his medications does not identify a reason for his altered mental status. The nursing staff has been unable to successfully redirect him to keep him calm. Which one of the following medications would be appropriate for the management of the patient's delirium?
questions.critique: Delirium is a syndrome characterized by an acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention, awareness, and cognition. Initial efforts should focus on identifying and addressing any underlying causes while providing redirection. However, if no reversible cause is found, managing delirium using medications becomes the most appropriate next step to ensure the patient's safety. While there are no FDA-approved medications for the management of delirium, second-generation antipsychotics such as olanzapine are preferred for this indication. Haloperidol is a less preferred option, compared to a second-generation antipsychotic. It is known to cause QT prolongation and other concerning side effects in older patients. Lithium is indicated for chronic management of bipolar disorder and does not have a role in the acute management of this patient's delirium. The side effects of benzodiazepines such as lorazepam make them undesirable for managing delirium, particularly in an older adult. Oxycodone is an opioid analgesic that would likely provide some degree of sedation but would not be appropriate for managing this patient's delirium.

questions.stem: An 84-year-old male with a history of coronary artery disease, mild cognitive impairment, and type 2 diabetes is admitted to your hospital for a hip fracture after a recent fall. On examination the patient has normal speech but is confused. He is clearly unable to articulate his understanding of the risks and benefits of surgery. You should do which one of the following to determine this patient's decision-making capacity regarding surgery?

options.A: Administer the Aid to Capacity Evaluation assessment tool
options.B: Administer the Montreal Cognitive Assessment evaluation tool
options.C: Document a lack of capacity and consult his designated durable power of attorney

questions.critique: This patient clearly lacks the capacity to make decisions, and a surrogate decision-maker should be consulted. If designated, a durable power of attorney for health care should make decisions for this patient. Structured assessments for determining decision-making capacity or cognitive function may help in uncertain cases but are not necessary in this situation. Psychiatry consultation is rarely necessary to determine decision-making capacity and would not be appropriate in this situation. Decision-making capacity is determined by the treating clinician, not the court.

questions.stem: A 36-year-old backcountry skier becomes separated from her group during a blizzard. The ski patrol locates her several hours later, sitting on the ground and not moving or shivering. She is conscious but confused. Which one of the following would be the most important initial step?

options.B: Providing a warm drink and high-calorie snack, and reconnecting her with her group
options.C: Having her remove her wet clothes and wrapping her in an insulated vapor barrier
options.D: Placing large heat pads around her upper body and wrapping her in an insulated vapor barrier
options.E: Performing emergency rapid sequence field intubation and preparing for evacuation

questions.critique: Hypothermia is a potentially life-threatening condition that physicians and first responders should be able to promptly recognize and treat. While core temperature is useful for academic purposes or in a hospital, it is impractical to obtain and unimportant in the field. A simplified hypothermia assessment for field use evaluates the patient's level of consciousness, presence or absence of shivering, level of alertness, and spontaneous movements to categorize the severity of hypothermia. This patient is not moving or shivering, and although she is conscious she is not alert. This patient is experiencing moderate hypothermia and requires immediate intervention. A patient with moderate hypothermia should be kept horizontal, have large warming packs placed in the axillae and around the upper chest, be wrapped in an insulated vapor barrier (the burrito method), and be prepared for transport to the nearest medical facility, ideally one with an ICU. Warm fluids and high-calorie foods can be used for a patient who is cold stressed or has mild hypothermia. Wet clothes should not be removed in the field unless shelter is available and rescue is more than 30 minutes away because this risks further lowering the patient's core temperature. Intubation is not necessary because the patient is protecting her airway and is conscious. Furthermore, it would not warm her.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old male presents for his annual health maintenance examination. He maintains a healthy lifestyle, eats well, and exercises regularly. A physical examination is normal. His most recent prostate cancer screening revealed a PSA level of 4.6 ng/mL (N 0.3–4.0), and his previous result 2 years ago was normal. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.critique: Prostate cancer screening recommendations vary, and there is little evidence that routine screening for prostate cancer improves patient-oriented outcomes. While the American Academy of Family Physicians does not recommend routine prostate-specific antigen (PSA)–based screening for prostate cancer, the American Urology Association (AUA) recommends prostate cancer screening with PSA testing for those 45–50 years of age. For those 50–69 years of age, the AUA recommends screening every 2–4 years between ages 50-69. The AUA also recommends obtaining a follow-up PSA level for individuals with newly elevated PSA before considering additional biomarkers, imaging, or biopsy. While this is based on expert opinion, the authors note that PSA may normalize in as many as 40% of individuals. The authors also suggest a higher threshold for biopsy based on age, with 4.5 ng/mL being the upper limit of normal for this patient's age group. The Choosing Wisely AUA initiative recommends against using empiric antibiotics to treat elevated PSA levels in asymptomatic individuals. A digital rectal examination is not recommended as a screening method alone or in addition to a screening PSA.

questions.stem: An otherwise healthy 37-year-old female presents because of a 4-day history of multiple episodes of watery, nonbloody diarrhea. She has no fever, chills, abdominal pain, or vomiting. She does not have a history of recent antibiotic use, hospitalization, or travel, but does say that her teenage son recently returned from a spring break trip and has similar symptoms. Physical examination findings include dry mucous membranes and tachycardia. The abdomen is soft with mild generalized tenderness on deep palpation. Which one of the following next steps would be most appropriate at this time?
questions.critique: Testing for fecal calprotectin, lactoferrin, and leukocytes is of limited value in evaluating a nontoxic patient with acute diarrhea, including infectious diarrhea. Testing for fecal occult blood and infection with Clostridioides difficile is also not recommended in these patients. Detecting fecal calprotectin in a patient with inflammatory bowel disease and bacterial diarrhea lacks consistency and is not recommended. Although the presence of fecal lactoferrin can be associated with a bacterial etiology, its lack of specificity makes it less useful. Fecal leukocytes disintegrate easily in stool samples, reducing test sensitivity. Indications for obtaining stool testing for acute infection include the presence of severe abdominal pain or tenderness, bloody or mucoid stools, clinical findings of sepsis, fever ≥38.3 °C (101.0 °F), immunocompromised state, recent hospitalization, recent antibiotic therapy, and diarrhea that lasts >7 days.

questions.stem: Which one of the following dog bite wounds should be considered for primary closure instead of being left to heal by secondary intention?

options.A: A crush injury to the arm
options.B: A gaping wound to the hand
options.C: A linear wound to the face
options.D: A puncture wound to the leg

questions.critique: Dog bite wounds may be primarily closed if desired for cosmetic reasons such as wounds to the face. However, dog bite wounds that carry a higher risk of infection such crush injuries, wounds to the hand, and puncture wounds, should be left to heal by secondary intention. Other factors that increase the risk of infection include delayed presentation (>6–12 hours for bites to the arm or leg and >12–24 hours for bites to the face) and bites in people who are immunosuppressed, including those with diabetes mellitus.
